time_period: Management appointments effective 02 April 2026; strategy work referenced "beyond" 2027 targets
body:
• Appointed Carol Howle as Deputy Chief Executive Officer, effective 02 April 2026.
  • Howle will continue to lead Supply, Trading & Shipping.
  • In the new role, Howle will oversee BP's ongoing portfolio review and strategic development.
  • BP notes Howle has spent 25 years with the company.
  • BP says Howle served as interim CEO in December 2025.
  • Meg O'Neill (described as CEO since 01 April 2026) said: "As I step into my new role I am committed to providing clear direction and consistency so our company can move forward with confidence."
  • Meg O'Neill said: "To support this, Carol will oversee the ongoing portfolio review and long-term strategy development beyond our 2027 targets. I'm looking forward to working closely with her to deliver a simpler, stronger, more valuable BP to maximize value for our shareholders."
summary: BP appoints Carol Howle as Deputy CEO effective April 2026

time_period: Motor finance redress scheme update (as at 2 April 2026)
body:
• Provision for the motor finance issue remains GBP1.95bn; Lloyds said it has "undertaken an assessment of the implications and impact of the final rules" and that "the group does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required."
  • FCA confirmed the industry-wide motor finance redress scheme on 30 March 2026.
  • FCA eligibility estimate tightened to 12.1m agreements (from 14.2m at consultation; -14.8%).
  • FCA expected total compensation ("redress") GBP7.5bn (from GBP8.2bn at consultation; -8.5%) and total industry cost (incl. admin) GBP9.1bn (from GBP11.0bn; -17.3%).
  • FCA says the scheme is being streamlined (incl. not requiring recorded delivery), claiming it cuts delivery cost to firms by >40%; also notes "in around 1 in 3 cases compensation will be capped."
  • Lloyds flagged residual uncertainty (response rates, litigation, operational costs): "The ultimate outcome may also differ dependent upon potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints. An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April."
  • Lloyds Q1 results date: 29 April 2026.
  • Share move on the day: -1.6% to 96.18p, but up >5% over the week.
  • Shore Capital view: stance "appears reasonable" given FCA tweaks, but the key risks remain litigation and take-up; Shore also highlights the FCA's assumed participation rate moving to 75% from 85% (-11.8%) and estimates expected remediation costs for banks down to ~GBP5.2bn from ~GBP5.6bn.
  • External/legal overhang cited in press: Black Horse exposure includes a reported lawsuit involving ~30,000 customers seeking GBP66m.
summary: FCA confirms motor finance redress scheme with reduced eligibility and compensation estimates

time_period: March 2026 traffic statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)
body:
• March passengers 15.8m (+5.3% YoY); load factor 93% (flat YoY).
  • March flights operated: 88,000+.
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 208.4m (+4.1% YoY); load factor 94% (flat YoY).
  • O'Leary on timing/risk of supply disruption: "But if the war continues, we do run the risk of supply disruptions in Europe in May and June and obviously we hope the war will finish sooner than that and that the risk to supply will be eliminated."
  • O'Leary on scenario outcomes: "It's almost impossible to know. If the war finishes and the Strait of Hormuz is opened by the middle or end of April, then there's no risk to supply."
  • O'Leary on potential scale of impact: "If the war continues, and the disruption to supply continues, we think there is a reasonable risk that maybe 10%, 20%, 25% of our supplies might be at risk through May and June."
  • O'Leary concluding: "So, like everybody else in the industry, we hope this war ends sooner rather than later."
  • Shares (early Thursday): -1.6% to EUR24.56.
  • Additional context from Reuters: Ryanair has warned prolonged conflict could force summer capacity reductions/cancellations if fuel availability tightens; it has also pointed to hedging and monitoring suppliers closely.
summary: Ryanair reports strong March passenger growth but warns of potential summer supply disruptions if conflict continues

time_period: March 2026 traffic and CO₂ emission statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)
body:
• March passengers 5.5m (+8.4% YoY).
  • March capacity +9.1% YoY to 6.12m seats.
  • March load factor 90.0% (from 90.5%; -0.5ppts YoY).
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 69.7m (+10% YoY).
  • Rolling 12-month load factor 90.7% (from 91.2%; -0.5ppts YoY).
  • CO₂ emissions per passenger-km: -2.7% YoY in March; -3.1% on a rolling 12-month basis.
  • Network/ops: continued expansion in Italy (adding aircraft for Milan/Rome/Venice) and opened a base at Podgorica (Montenegro).
  • Wizz made no comment on fuel supply risk in this update.
  • Shares (early Thursday): -4.0% to 866.50p.
summary: Wizz Air March passengers grew 8.4% despite load factor declining slightly to 90.0%

time_period: Director dealings (shares bought on 1 April 2026; reported 2 April 2026)
body:
• Jane Eady (wife of CFO Neil Eady) bought 1,750 shares at GBP28.86 (value GBP50,497).
  • Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at GBP28.78 (value GBP201,482).
  • Share weakness catalyst cited: Berkeley said it has decided to halt land buying amid a UK housing market downturn it linked to the Middle East conflict; it also cited an "unprecedented" increase in UK cost/regulation.
  • Reported reference prices: 3,134.00p (+1.0% on the Thursday); 12-month change -12%.
summary: Berkeley Group executives buy shares despite halting land purchases amid housing downturn

time_period: Broker note / forward outlook (2026–2027)
body:
• Peel Hunt downgraded to 'hold' from 'buy', with price target unchanged at 270p.
  • Broker rationale: upgrade to medium-term production outlook (stable 2026; +3% growth in 2027 vs prior), but rating cut after a sharp share-price move.
  • Related company context: Harbour reported record 2025 production of 474 kboepd (+84% YoY) and refreshed its shareholder returns framework (linking returns to free cash flow/leverage).
summary: Peel Hunt downgrades Harbour Energy to hold despite raising production outlook

time_period: Board change effective 31 May 2026
body:
• Barbara Moorhouse will step down as a Non-executive Director on 31 May 2026 after nine years on the board.
  • Chair quote (Charles Allen, Lord Allen of Kensington, CBE): "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Barbara for her outstanding contribution to Balfour Beatty over the past nine years. Barbara has brought great insight, rigour and sound judgement to her role, and her service on the Audit and Risk, Remuneration and Nomination Committees has been highly valued. We are grateful for her significant commitment and dedication, and we wish her every success in the future."
summary: Barbara Moorhouse stepping down as Non-executive Director at Balfour Beatty in May 2026

time_period: Interim CFO departure (effective 2 April 2026) and leaver terms (FY ending June 2026)
body:
• Marie Wall stepped down from the Board and as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 (following the 20 Jan 2026 announcement).
  • Marie received salary, pension and benefits up to 2 April 2026, plus a payment in lieu of accrued but untaken holiday.
  • Annual bonus: treated as a good leaver; eligible for an annual pro-rata bonus for the financial year ending June 2026, subject to Remuneration Committee approval, paid at the normal payment date.
  • Share plan: awards under the DFS Group Share Plan lapse on leaving; includes a grant of 182,724 shares.
  • Disclosure: remuneration details to be included in the Directors' Remuneration Report in the Annual Report for the year ending 28 June 2026.
summary: Marie Wall resigned as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 with pro-rata bonus eligibility

body:
Time Period Motor finance redress scheme update (as at 2 April 2026)

• Provision for the motor finance issue remains GBP1.95bn; Lloyds said it has “undertaken an assessment of the implications and impact of the final rules” and that “the group does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required,” ()

• FCA confirmed the industry-wide motor finance redress scheme on 30 March 2026. ()

• FCA eligibility estimate tightened to 12.1m agreements (from 14.2m at consultation; -14.8%). ()

• FCA expected total compensation (“redress”) GBP7.5bn (from GBP8.2bn at consultation; -8.5%) and total industry cost (incl. admin) GBP9.1bn (from GBP11.0bn; -17.3%). ()

• FCA says the scheme is being streamlined (incl. not requiring recorded delivery), claiming it cuts delivery cost to firms by >40%; also notes “in around 1 in 3 cases compensation will be capped.” ()

• Lloyds flagged residual uncertainty (response rates, litigation, operational costs): “The ultimate outcome may also differ dependent upon potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints. An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April,” ()

• Lloyds Q1 results date: 29 April 2026. ()

• Share move on the day: -1.6% to 96.18p, but up >5% over the week (per the report). ()

• Shore Capital view (via Proactive): stance “appears reasonable” given FCA tweaks, but the key risks remain litigation and take-up; Shore also highlights the FCA’s assumed participation rate moving to 75% from 85% (-11.8%) and estimates expected remediation costs for banks down to ~GBP5.2bn from ~GBP5.6bn (non-banks benefiting more). ()

• Earlier Lloyds positioning after FCA decision: “The details of the final scheme differ from the scheme as laid out in October 2025 and require careful analysis. Accordingly, the group is assessing the implications and impact of the final rules. The group will update the market as and when appropriate,” ()

• External/legal overhang cited in press: Black Horse exposure includes a reported lawsuit involving ~30,000 customers seeking GBP66m (per reporting referenced alongside this update). ()

Time Period As at 31 March 2026

• Share capital (one class): 103,461,185 ordinary shares in issue (par value £0.0001 each); no treasury shares.

• Total voting rights denominator: 103,461,185.

• Voting rights caveat: shares held by Non-Qualifying Nationals subject to proportional disenfranchisement (most recently referenced by the company on 25 June 2025).

• “Theoretical fully diluted” share capital stated at 127,769,507, comprising:

• 24,246,715 shares from full conversion of outstanding convertible notes (excluding any shares for accrued/unpaid interest).

body:
Time Period March 2026 (and rolling 12 months to March 2026)

• March passengers: 5.51m vs 5.08m (+8.4% YoY).

• March capacity (seats): 6.12m vs 5.61m (+9.1% YoY).

• March load factor: 90.0% vs 90.5% (-0.5 ppts YoY).

• Rolling 12-month passengers: 69.75m vs 63.40m (+10.0% YoY).

• Rolling 12-month capacity (seats): 76.88m vs 69.55m (+10.5% YoY).

• Rolling 12-month load factor: 90.7% vs 91.2% (-0.4 ppts YoY).

• Operational note: delivered March growth “despite Middle East cuts” (no further quantification given).

• Base update: Târgu Mureș base reopened 31 March with one A320neo; now 10 routes to 8 countries (examples listed include Milan Bergamo, Rome Fiumicino, Brussels Charleroi, Paris Beauvais, Dortmund, Memmingen, London Luton).

• Base update: Podgorica base opened 30 March; now 17 international routes from the capital (positioned as making Montenegro a “key market”).

• Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 10th based aircraft at Milan Malpensa and 17th at Rome Fiumicino (both from October 2026).

• Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 4th based aircraft at Venice from September 2026.

• Italy market share: originating seats market share ~11% for FY ending March 2026, ranked #2 overall (per Innovata + Wizz data).

• New ancillary stream: rolled out Bluetooth-enabled in-seat ordering across the fleet via the mobile app (browse/purchase onboard even without internet access).

• March CO2 emissions: 463,585 MT vs 467,497 MT (-0.8% YoY).

• March CO2 intensity: 49.6 g/pax/km vs 51.0 (-2.7% YoY).

• Rolling 12-month CO2 emissions: 6,060,538 MT vs 5,796,439 MT (+4.6% YoY).

• Rolling 12-month CO2 intensity: 50.6 g/pax/km vs 52.2 (-3.1% YoY).
summary: Wizz Air grew passengers 8.4% in March while expanding Italian operations and launching new bases

body:
Time Period Transaction completion (effective 01 April 2026)

• Completed its long-term silver streaming transaction with Wheaton Precious Metals covering BHP’s share of silver production from the Antamina Mine (Peru). ()

• BHP received US$4.3bn total upfront consideration.

• In exchange, BHP will transfer to Wheaton the equivalent of 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to a fixed payable rate of 90%), plus ongoing production transfer payments set at 20% of the spot silver price per ounce delivered under the agreement. ()

• After 100 million ounces have been delivered to Wheaton, the stream reduces to 22.5% of Antamina silver production for the remaining life of mine.

• Settlement is via metal credits (i.e. no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton).
summary: BHP completes $4.3bn silver streaming deal with Wheaton Precious Metals at Antamina

• Approved allotment of 3,426,403 new ordinary shares (€0.001 each) to satisfy vesting of employee share awards under the Cairn Homes plc Long Term Incentive Plan.

• Application made for the new shares to be admitted to trading on Euronext Dublin and the UK Official List; admission expected 07 April 2026.

body:
Time Period High Court trial in February 2026; judgment issued 01 April 2026 ()

• easyGroup brought a trademark claim against Easyfeetstore (Estonia) and Easyfeet Inc (US) (orthopaedic/orthotic insoles), plus shareholder/director Andriy Klishyn. ()

• Judge Richard Hacon dismissed the claim; found “easy” was the only similarity and there was “no likelihood” of confusion. ()

• Judge: “I have found that there is no relevant similarity…” and (for six of seven trademarks) “no similarities in goods or services.” ()

• Judge also found no “detriment” to easyGroup and no “unfair advantage”. ()

• Context noted: easyGroup has 380+ “easy” branded ventures/websites (easyJet, easyBus, easyHotel etc.) and has pursued multiple “easy” trademark disputes. ()

• Company response / appeal intent (spokesperson quote): “We strongly believe that this judgment is wrong because it undermines the ability of owners of famous families of brands, such as the easy family of brands, to protect consumers from confusion.

“The other side wants consumers to believe that they have something to do with the official easy family of brands when they do not.

“We will appeal and will overturn it, as we have done many times in the past.”
summary: easyGroup loses trademark case against Easyfeetstore, plans to appeal judgment

body:
Time Period Transaction completion (effective date 1 April 2026)

• BHP confirmed it has completed the long-term silver streaming agreement with Wheaton (following the earlier announcement on 17 Feb 2026). ()

• BHP has received the US$4.3bn upfront consideration from Wheaton. ()

• In return, BHP will transfer exposure equivalent to 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to 90% payable rate) until 100Moz delivered; thereafter stream reduces to 22.5%. ()

• Ongoing production transfer payments are 20% of spot silver price per ounce delivered. ()

• Mechanics: “The settlement of the stream is completed via metal credits with no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton.” ()

Carol Howle appointed Deputy CEO to oversee portfolio review and long-term strategy
body: Time Period Management appointments effective 02 April 2026; strategy work referenced “beyond” 2027 targets • Appointed Carol Howle as Deputy Chief Executive Officer, effective 02 April 2026. • Howle will continue to lead Supply, Trading & Shipping. • In the new role, Howle will oversee BP’s ongoing portfolio review and strategic development. • BP notes Howle has spent 25 years with the company. • BP says Howle served as interim CEO in December 2025. • Meg O’Neill (described as CEO since 01 April 2026) said: “As I step into my new role I am committed to providing clear direction and consistency so our company can move forward with confidence.” • Meg O’Neill said: “To support this, Carol will oversee the ongoing portfolio review and long-term strategy development beyond our 2027 targets. I'm looking forward to working closely with her to deliver a simpler, stronger, more valuable BP to maximize value for our shareholders.”

summary: Lloyds maintains £1.95bn motor finance provision as FCA confirms streamlined redress scheme
body: Time Period Motor finance redress scheme update (as at 2 April 2026) • Provision for the motor finance issue remains GBP1.95bn; Lloyds said it has “undertaken an assessment of the implications and impact of the final rules” and that “the group does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required,” () • FCA confirmed the industry-wide motor finance redress scheme on 30 March 2026. () • FCA eligibility estimate tightened to 12.1m agreements (from 14.2m at consultation; -14.8%). () • FCA expected total compensation (“redress”) GBP7.5bn (from GBP8.2bn at consultation; -8.5%) and total industry cost (incl. admin) GBP9.1bn (from GBP11.0bn; -17.3%). () • FCA says the scheme is being streamlined (incl. not requiring recorded delivery), claiming it cuts delivery cost to firms by >40%; also notes “in around 1 in 3 cases compensation will be capped.” () • Lloyds flagged residual uncertainty (response rates, litigation, operational costs): “The ultimate outcome may also differ dependent upon potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints. An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April,” () • Lloyds Q1 results date: 29 April 2026. () • Share move on the day: -1.6% to 96.18p, but up >5% over the week (per the report). () • Shore Capital view (via Proactive): stance “appears reasonable” given FCA tweaks, but the key risks remain litigation and take-up; Shore also highlights the FCA’s assumed participation rate moving to 75% from 85% (-11.8%) and estimates expected remediation costs for banks down to ~GBP5.2bn from ~GBP5.6bn (non-banks benefiting more). () • Earlier Lloyds positioning after FCA decision: “The details of the final scheme differ from the scheme as laid out in October 2025 and require careful analysis. Accordingly, the group is assessing the implications and impact of the final rules. The group will update the market as and when appropriate,” () • External/legal overhang cited in press: Black Horse exposure includes a reported lawsuit involving ~30,000 customers seeking GBP66m (per reporting referenced alongside this update). ()

summary: Ryanair reports 5.3% March passenger growth but warns of potential supply disruption risks
body: Time Period March 2026 traffic statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026) • March passengers 15.8m (+5.3% YoY); load factor 93% (flat YoY). () • March flights operated: 88,000+. () • Rolling 12-month passengers 208.4m (+4.1% YoY); load factor 94% (flat YoY). () • O’Leary (Sky News) on timing/risk of supply disruption: “But if the war continues, we do run the risk of supply disruptions in Europe in May and June and obviously we hope the war will finish sooner than that and that the risk to supply will be eliminated,” () • O’Leary on scenario outcomes: “It’s almost impossible to know. If the war finishes and the Strait of Hormuz is opened by the middle or end of April, then there’s no risk to supply.” () • O’Leary on potential scale of impact: “If the war continues, and the disruption to supply continues, we think there is a reasonable risk that maybe 10%, 20%, 25% of our supplies might be at risk through May and June.” () • O’Leary concluding: “So, like everybody else in the industry, we hope this war ends sooner rather than later.” () • Shares (early Thursday): -1.6% to EUR24.56. () • Additional context from Reuters: Ryanair has warned prolonged conflict could force summer capacity reductions/cancellations if fuel availability tightens; it has also pointed to hedging and monitoring suppliers closely. ()

summary: Wizz Air reports 8.4% passenger growth in March despite slight load factor decline
body: Time Period March 2026 traffic and CO₂ emission statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026) • March passengers 5.5m (+8.4% YoY). () • March capacity +9.1% YoY to 6.12m seats. () • March load factor 90.0% (from 90.5%; -0.5ppts YoY). () • Rolling 12-month passengers 69.7m (+10% YoY). () • Rolling 12-month load factor 90.7% (from 91.2%; -0.5ppts YoY). summary: Berkeley Group executives buy shares despite housing market downturn and halted land purchases

summary: Peel Hunt downgrades Harbour Energy to hold despite upgraded production outlook
body: Time Period Broker note / forward outlook (2026–2027) • Peel Hunt downgraded to ‘hold’ from ‘buy’, with price target unchanged at 270p. () • Broker rationale in the note you provided: upgrade to medium-term production outlook (stable 2026; +3% growth in 2027 vs prior), but rating cut after a sharp share-price move. • Related company context (for anchoring): Harbour reported record 2025 production of 474 kboepd (+84% YoY) and refreshed its shareholder returns framework (linking returns to free cash flow/leverage). ()

summary: Barbara Moorhouse steps down as Non-executive Director after nine years at Balfour Beatty
body: Time Period Board change effective 31 May 2026 • Barbara Moorhouse will step down as a Non-executive Director on 31 May 2026 after nine years on the board. () • Chair quote (Charles Allen, Lord Allen of Kensington, CBE): “On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Barbara for her outstanding contribution to Balfour Beatty over the past nine years. Barbara has brought great insight, rigour and sound judgement to her role, and her service on the Audit and Risk, Remuneration and Nomination Committees has been highly valued. We are grateful for her significant commitment and dedication, and we wish her every success in the future.” ()

summary: Marie Wall departed as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 with standard leaver terms
body: Time Period Interim CFO departure (effective 2 April 2026) and leaver terms (FY ending June 2026) • Marie Wall stepped down from the Board and as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 (following the 20 Jan 2026 announcement). () • Marie received salary, pension and benefits up to 2 April 2026, plus a payment in lieu of accrued but untaken holiday. () • Annual bonus: treated as a good leaver; eligible for an annual pro-rata bonus for the financial year ending June 2026, subject to Remuneration Committee approval, paid at the normal payment date. () • Share plan: awards under the DFS Group Share Plan lapse on leaving; includes a grant of 182,724 shares. () • Disclosure: remuneration details to be included in the Directors’ Remuneration Report in the Annual Report for the year ending 28 June 2026; the Section 430(2B) info remains on the website until the next Remuneration Report is published. ()

summary: Wizz Air has 103.5 million shares issued with potential 23.5% dilution from convertibles
body: Time Period As at 31 March 2026 • Share capital (one class): 103,461,185 ordinary shares in issue (par value £0.0001 each); no treasury shares. • Total voting rights denominator: 103,461,185. • Voting rights caveat: shares held by Non-Qualifying Nationals subject to proportional disenfranchisement (most recently referenced by the company on 25 June 2025). • “Theoretical fully diluted” share capital stated at 127,769,507, comprising: • 24,246,715 shares from full conversion of outstanding convertible notes (excluding any shares for accrued/unpaid interest). • 61,607 shares from vested-but-unexercised employee options. • Implied incremental dilution versus current issued shares if fully diluted: +24,308,322 shares (i.e. ~23.5% uplift vs current issued share count).

summary: Wizz Air grew passengers 8.4% in March while expanding Italian bases and launching new ancillary services
body: Time Period March 2026 (and rolling 12 months to March 2026) • March passengers: 5.51m vs 5.08m (+8.4% YoY). • March capacity (seats): 6.12m vs 5.61m (+9.1% YoY). • March load factor: 90.0% vs 90.5% (-0.5 ppts YoY). • Rolling 12-month passengers: 69.75m vs 63.40m (+10.0% YoY). • Rolling 12-month capacity (seats): 76.88m vs 69.55m (+10.5% YoY). • Rolling 12-month load factor: 90.7% vs 91.2% (-0.4 ppts YoY). • Operational note: delivered March growth “despite Middle East cuts” (no further quantification given). • Base update: Târgu Mureș base reopened 31 March with one A320neo; now 10 routes to 8 countries (examples listed include Milan Bergamo, Rome Fiumicino, Brussels Charleroi, Paris Beauvais, Dortmund, Memmingen, London Luton). • Base update: Podgorica base opened 30 March; now 17 international routes from the capital (positioned as making Montenegro a “key market”). • Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 10th based aircraft at Milan Malpensa and 17th at Rome Fiumicino (both from October 2026). • Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 4th based aircraft at Venice from September 2026. • Italy market share: originating seats market share ~11% for FY ending March 2026, ranked #2 overall (per Innovata + Wizz data). • New ancillary stream: rolled out Bluetooth-enabled in-seat ordering across the fleet via the mobile app (browse/purchase onboard even without internet access). • March CO2 emissions: 463,585 MT vs 467,497 MT (-0.8% YoY). • March CO2 intensity: 49.6 g/pax/km vs 51.0 (-2.7% YoY). • Rolling 12-month CO2 emissions: 6,060,538 MT vs 5,796,439 MT (+4.6% YoY). • Rolling 12-month CO2 intensity: 50.6 g/pax/km vs 52.2 (-3.1% YoY).

summary: Lloyds maintains current motor finance redress provision despite FCA scheme rules
body: Time Period N/A (regulatory/provisioning update) • Following the FCA’s publication of final rules for an industry-wide motor finance redress scheme, Lloyds says it has assessed implications and impact and: “does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required.” () • Lloyds flags ongoing uncertainties that could change the ultimate cost: response rates, operational costs, litigation, and “potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints.” • Lloyds: “An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April.” • Lloyds: “The Group remains committed to ensuring customers receive appropriate and timely redress.” • External context on the FCA scheme (for sizing): FCA indicates the scheme covers motor finance agreements in the 2007–2024 period and expects ~12.1m agreements to be in-scope, with average redress around ~£830 per agreement and total compensation estimated around ~£7.5bn (with broader industry cost estimates higher once costs are included). ()

summary: BHP receives $4.3bn upfront for long-term silver streaming deal with Wheaton Precious Metals
body: Time Period Transaction completion (effective 01 April 2026) • Completed its long-term silver streaming transaction with Wheaton Precious Metals covering BHP’s share of silver production from the Antamina Mine (Peru). () • BHP received US$4.3bn total upfront consideration. • In exchange, BHP will transfer to Wheaton the equivalent of 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to a fixed payable rate of 90%), plus ongoing production transfer payments set at 20% of the spot silver price per ounce delivered under the agreement. () • After 100 million ounces have been delivered to Wheaton, the stream reduces to 22.5% of Antamina silver production for the remaining life of mine. • Settlement is via metal credits (i.e. no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton).

summary: Cairn Homes allots 3.4m shares for employee incentive plan awards
body: Time Period N/A (equity issue announcement) • Approved allotment of 3,426,403 new ordinary shares (€0.001 each) to satisfy vesting of employee share awards under the Cairn Homes plc Long Term Incentive Plan. • Application made for the new shares to be admitted to trading on Euronext Dublin and the UK Official List; admission expected 07 April 2026. • New shares will rank pari passu with existing shares. • Total issued share capital increases to 629,002,525 ordinary shares (implies pre-allotment shares of 625,576,122), i.e. share count up ~0.55%.

summary: High Court dismisses easyGroup's trademark claim against Easyfeetstore and Easyfeet Inc
body:
Time Period High Court trial in February 2026; judgment issued 01 April 2026 () • easyGroup brought a trademark claim against Easyfeetstore (Estonia) and Easyfeet Inc (US) (orthopaedic/orthotic insoles), plus shareholder/director Andriy Klishyn. () • Judge Richard Hacon dismissed the claim; found “easy” was the only similarity and there was “no likelihood” of confusion. () • Judge: “I have found that there is no relevant similarity…” and (for six of seven trademarks) “no similarities in goods or services.” () • Judge also found no “detriment” to easyGroup and no “unfair advantage”. () • Context noted: easyGroup has 380+ “easy” branded ventures/websites (easyJet, easyBus, easyHotel etc.) and has pursued multiple “easy” trademark disputes. () • Company response / appeal intent (spokesperson quote): “We strongly believe that this judgment is wrong because it undermines the ability of owners of famous families of brands, such as the easy family of brands, to protect consumers from confusion. “The other side wants consumers to believe that they have something to do with the official easy family of brands when they do not.

“We will appeal and will overturn it, as we have done many times in the past.”

summary: BHP completes $4.3bn silver streaming deal with Wheaton at Antamina
body: Time Period Transaction completion (effective date 1 April 2026) • BHP confirmed it has completed the long-term silver streaming agreement with Wheaton (following the earlier announcement on 17 Feb 2026). () • BHP has received the US$4.3bn upfront consideration from Wheaton. () • In return, BHP will transfer exposure equivalent to 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to 90% payable rate) until 100Moz delivered; thereafter stream reduces to 22.5%. () • Ongoing production transfer payments are 20% of spot silver price per ounce delivered. () • Mechanics: “The settlement of the stream is completed via metal credits with no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton.” ()

body: Time Period March 2026 (and rolling 12 months to March 2026) • March passengers: 5.51m vs 5.08m (+8.4% YoY). • March capacity (seats): 6.12m vs 5.61m (+9.1% YoY). • March load factor: 90.0% vs 90.5% (-0.5 ppts YoY). • Rolling 12-month passengers: 69.75m vs 63.40m (+10.0% YoY). • Rolling 12-month capacity (seats): 76.88m vs 69.55m (+10.5% YoY). • Rolling 12-month load factor: 90.7% vs 91.2% (-0.4 ppts YoY). • Operational note: delivered March growth “despite Middle East cuts” (no further quantification given). • Base update: Târgu Mureș base reopened 31 March with one A320neo; now 10 routes to 8 countries (examples listed include Milan Bergamo, Rome Fiumicino, Brussels Charleroi, Paris Beauvais, Dortmund, Memmingen, London Luton). • Base update: Podgorica base opened 30 March; now 17 international routes from the capital (positioned as making Montenegro a “key market”). • Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 10th based aircraft at Milan Malpensa and 17th at Rome Fiumicino (both from October 2026). • Italy fleet allocation: confirmed 4th based aircraft at Venice from September 2026. • Italy market share: originating seats market share ~11% for FY ending March 2026, ranked #2 overall (per Innovata + Wizz data). • New ancillary stream: rolled out Bluetooth-enabled in-seat ordering across the fleet via the mobile app (browse/purchase onboard even without internet access). • March CO2 emissions: 463,585 MT vs 467,497 MT (-0.8% YoY). • March CO2 intensity: 49.6 g/pax/km vs 51.0 (-2.7% YoY). • Rolling 12-month CO2 emissions: 6,060,538 MT vs 5,796,439 MT (+4.6% YoY). • Rolling 12-month CO2 intensity: 50.6 g/pax/km vs 52.2 (-3.1% YoY).

summary: BHP secured $4.3bn upfront by streaming 33.75% of Antamina silver production to Wheaton
body: Time Period Transaction completion (effective 01 April 2026) • Completed its long-term silver streaming transaction with Wheaton Precious Metals covering BHP’s share of silver production from the Antamina Mine (Peru). () • BHP received US$4.3bn total upfront consideration. • In exchange, BHP will transfer to Wheaton the equivalent of 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to a fixed payable rate of 90%), plus ongoing production transfer payments set at 20% of the spot silver price per ounce delivered under the agreement. () • After 100 million ounces have been delivered to Wheaton, the stream reduces to 22.5% of Antamina silver production for the remaining life of mine. • Settlement is via metal credits (i.e. no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton).

summary: Cairn Homes issues 3.4 million new shares for employee incentive plan vesting
body: Time Period N/A (equity issue announcement) • Approved allotment of 3,426,403 new ordinary shares (€0.001 each) to satisfy vesting of employee share awards under the Cairn Homes plc Long Term Incentive Plan. • Application made for the new shares to be admitted to trading on Euronext Dublin and the UK Official List; admission expected 07 April 2026. • New shares will rank pari passu with existing shares. • Total issued share capital increases to 629,002,525 ordinary shares (implies pre-allotment shares of 625,576,122), i.e. share count up ~0.55%.

summary: High Court dismisses easyGroup trademark claim against Easyfeetstore and Easyfeet Inc
body:
Time Period High Court trial in February 2026; judgment issued 01 April 2026 () • easyGroup brought a trademark claim against Easyfeetstore (Estonia) and Easyfeet Inc (US) (orthopaedic/orthotic insoles), plus shareholder/director Andriy Klishyn. () • Judge Richard Hacon dismissed the claim; found “easy” was the only similarity and there was “no likelihood” of confusion. () • Judge: “I have found that there is no relevant similarity…” and (for six of seven trademarks) “no similarities in goods or services.” () • Judge also found no “detriment” to easyGroup and no “unfair advantage”. () • Context noted: easyGroup has 380+ “easy” branded ventures/websites (easyJet, easyBus, easyHotel etc.) and has pursued multiple “easy” trademark disputes. () • Company response / appeal intent (spokesperson quote): “We strongly believe that this judgment is wrong because it undermines the ability of owners of famous families of brands, such as the easy family of brands, to protect consumers from confusion. “The other side wants consumers to believe that they have something to do with the official easy family of brands when they do not.

“We will appeal and will overturn it, as we have done many times in the past.”

summary: BHP completes $4.3bn silver streaming deal with Wheaton on Antamina production
body: Time Period Transaction completion (effective date 1 April 2026) • BHP confirmed it has completed the long-term silver streaming agreement with Wheaton (following the earlier announcement on 17 Feb 2026). () • BHP has received the US$4.3bn upfront consideration from Wheaton. () • In return, BHP will transfer exposure equivalent to 33.75% of Antamina silver production (subject to 90% payable rate) until 100Moz delivered; thereafter stream reduces to 22.5%. () • Ongoing production transfer payments are 20% of spot silver price per ounce delivered. () • Mechanics: “The settlement of the stream is completed via metal credits with no physical delivery of silver to Wheaton.” ()

summary: Aurore Blair purchases nearly 49,000 Trustpilot shares for approximately £99,349
body: On 2 April, Aurore Blair (closely associated with CEO Adrian Blair) bought 48,844 shares at 203.4p average, total GBP99,349.

• Announced final results of its “modified Dutch Auction” tender offer, which expired “at one (1) minute after 11:59 P.M., New York City time, on April 1, 2026.”

• Deposit count (Equiniti Trust Company, LLC): 17,577,250 ordinary shares were properly tendered and not properly withdrawn at or below $92.00 per share.

• Notes that not all shares tendered via notice of guaranteed delivery were delivered within the required settlement period.

• Accepted for payment 17,577,250 shares (all properly tendered and not properly withdrawn) at $92.00 per share.

• Aggregate repurchase cost stated as ~$1.617 billion, excluding fees and expenses.

• Repurchased shares represent ~29.7% of shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026.

• Implied total shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026 are ~59.2 million (based on 17.577m being 29.7%).

• Nir Zohar, President and Co-founder, stated: “I am very pleased with the outcome of our Dutch auction tender offer, which is enabling us to repurchase nearly 30% of our market capitalization and return meaningful value to investors. We believe the results of this transaction reflect shared conviction among our broader shareholder base in our long-term strateg”
summary: Wix completes $1.6 billion share repurchase of nearly 30% outstanding shares

body:
Time Period FCA motor finance redress scheme confirmed 30 March 2026; company reaction 1 April 2026

• Welcomed the FCA’s final motor finance redress approach, saying it expects the proposals to halve the number of Advantage customers eligible for redress. ()

• S&U emphasised Advantage never offered discretionary commission arrangements (DCAs) (the core issue cited by the FCA). ()

• Karl Werner (CEO of Advantage) (full quote): “Advantage has never offered DCA commissions or engaged in tied arrangements. Our commission levels as a proportion of the charge for credit are generally well within those proposed for redress by the FCA and only exceed them for very small loans. Even before the final rules were published nearly 98% of Advantage customers did not qualify. We anticipate these finalised FCA proposals to halve the number of our customers that are eligible for redress under the scheme.” ()

• Werner (full quote): “Advantage also welcomed the simplification of the redress process, particularly on how customers should be contacted. The company also agreed with the FCA's proposal that the grounds for rebutting claims should include cases where there was no better deal available for the customer.” ()

• Werner (full quote): “Although a redress scheme of any kind is not justified for Advantage in our view, we are pleased that this matter can now be concluded by the early Autumn. The provisions we have made for its costs will prove more than adequate. It should therefore in no way impede the ambitious plans we now have in place for building Advantage.” ()

• FCA scheme size: regulator estimates ~12.1m agreements eligible (down ~15% from 14.2m proposed earlier). ()

• FCA cost estimate: ~£7.5bn compensation; ~£9.1bn total including admin (down from earlier estimates up to ~£11bn). ()

• Additional FCA detail from coverage: average payout cited around ~£830 per eligible agreement. ()
summary: FCA motor finance redress scheme finalised, S&U expects eligible Advantage customers to halve

body:
Time Period Q1 2026 trading update (with cash position at 31 March 2026); FY2025 accounts timing update

• Operations were suspended after attacks on Ukrainian energy infrastructure in Jan 2026; production restarted in Feb 2026, with one pellet line currently operating. ()

• Cost/working-capital actions: reduced working time, cuts to procurement, and continued suspension of all non-essential capex, overheads, and CSR spending. ()

• VAT refunds: withholding continued in Q1 2026; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be ~US$80m at end-March 2026. ()

• Liquidity: available cash US$35m at 31 Mar 2026; no debt other than lease liabilities; net cash ~US$25m (vs US$47m at 31 Dec 2025 and US$30m at 27 Feb 2026). ()

• Accessibility of cash: excluding funds held with MBaer Merchant Bank, net accessible cash ~US$22m at 31 Mar 2026. ()

• Cash runway: based on forecast cash burn, expects sufficient cash only until at least end-June 2026. ()

• Funding: “actively exploring” options that “could include an equity capital raise”; “no certainty” of success; warns continued VAT withholding + unresolved funding could lead to “material negative consequences”. ()

• FY2025 accounts: needs more time to finalise, but expects to report on or before 30 April 2026.
summary: Ferrexpo faces severe liquidity crisis with cash runway until June 2026 amid Ukrainian operations disruption

body:
Time Period Bridging agreement effective from 1 April 2026 (following expiry of prior contract on 31 March 2026)

• Signed a six-month bridging agreement with the UK Ministry of Defence under the Future Maritime Support Programme (FMSP), after the prior five-year FMSP support contract ended on 31 March 2026. ()

• Babcock said it is “in the latter stages of negotiation with the MoD” on a new long-term agreement. ()

• Company statement (full quote): “The contract bridges to a new long-term agreement which is in the latter stages of negotiation with the MOD. A Letter of Intent from the MOD sits alongside the bridging agreement and reinforces the commitment to a long-term strategic relationship between the MOD, the Royal Navy and Babcock, and the critical contribution that Babcock makes to the UK defence capability, including as the sole provider of in-service submarine support to the Royal Navy”. ()

• Company statement (full quote): “The future agreement supports the government and Royal Navy's priorities and aligns with the strategic defence review and defence industrial strategy.” ()

• Prior five-year FMSP contract was agreed in 2021 and was worth ~£3.5bn (per the company/background coverage). ()

• New long-term deal is expected to support “increased demand, scope and operations” at Clyde and Devonport naval bases (Clyde hosts the UK’s nuclear deterrent). ()

• CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “The strength of Babcock's partnership with the Ministry of Defence and Royal Navy is critical to the increased availability and resilience of the UK's submarine fleet and the defence of our nation.” ()

• CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “This agreement with the Submarine Delivery Agency reinforces our joint intention to move to a new long-term agreement that will see even more investment in skills, communities and infrastructure, while growing this critical, sovereign capability, together.”
summary: Babcock secures six-month bridging agreement with UK Ministry of Defence while negotiating long-term FMSP contract

body:
Time Period Disposal announced 1 April 2026; completion scheduled for June 2026

• Agreed sale of Horseferry House, Westminster (Burberry HQ) to an “overseas investor” for £131.8m (before rental top-ups); completion expected June 2026. ()

• Sale price is slightly below the December (2025) book value (i.e., marginally under 31 Dec 2025 valuation). ()

• Derwent bought Horseferry House in 2005 for £34m; headline sale price implies ~3.9x the purchase price (c. +288% vs 2005 cost, ignoring capex/holding costs). ()

• Lease action (full quote): “Burberry's lease was regeared, extending the term by five years to 2043 (no breaks) and incorporating two new five-yearly fixed uplifts in 2033 and 2038”. ()

• Derwent has now exchanged contracts on ~£275m of sales since the start of 2026; in talks for a further ~£100m, towards its £1bn three-year disposals target. ()
summary: Derwent London sells Horseferry House to overseas investor for £131.8m in June 2026

company: Saltchuk Resources, Inc. / Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation (GLDD)
body:
Time Period Transaction closed 01 April 2026; tender offer expired late 31 March 2026 ()

• Acquisition closed: Great Lakes became Saltchuk’s newest wholly owned subsidiary; $17.00/share cash, enterprise value ~$1.5bn. ()

• Quote (Mark Tabbutt, Saltchuk Chairman): “We're proud to welcome Great Lakes to the Saltchuk family of companies… With Great Lakes, we have grown to nearly 10,000 team members… We look forward to supporting Great Lakes’ reinvestment and growth ambitions for generations to come.” ()

• Operating model: Great Lakes to join as a stand-alone business unit, continuing to operate independently under current leadership. ()

• Quote (Lasse Petterson, GLDD CEO): “Joining Saltchuk’s family of companies is a proud moment for us… This partnership represents a natural alignment of values and vision…”

• Quote (Petterson – strategy): “As we look ahead, we remain focused on executing our long-term growth strategy with discipline and purpose… committed to maintaining and enhancing our leadership position in U.S. dredging, the global offshore energy sector…”

• Tender offer expiry: one minute after 11:59pm New York time on 31 March 2026. ()

• Shares tendered: 53,738,558 shares tendered and not withdrawn = 79.88% of issued/outstanding GLDD shares. ()

• Listing impact: before NASDAQ open on 01 April 2026, GLDD shares cease trading and then are delisted and deregistered. ()
summary: Saltchuk acquires Great Lakes Dredge & Dock for $1.5 billion in all-cash deal

body:
Time Period Unscheduled trading update (FY ending 30 April 2026; outlook through FY2030)

• Will stop acquiring new land and reduce work-in-progress investment to match current sales levels, citing the Iran war and deteriorating economic backdrop. ()

• Company framing (full quote): taking “decisive action to maximise long term shareholder value” by re-phasing delivery of the “Berkeley 2035” strategy over the four years to 2030. ()

• Company framing (full quote): “With the ongoing conflict and deterioration of the economic outlook, we are reducing work in progress investment to match the sales levels we are currently achieving. We are forecasting we can absorb the expected cost inflation through optimisation of our land holdings”. ()

• Said it won’t acquire new land while conditions prevail as it “does not believe it can make its required rate of return on investment.” ()

• Now guides to >£1.4bn pretax profit across FY2027–FY2030; RBC cited in your text as ~29% below Visible Alpha consensus £1.98bn (implies ~£0.58bn shortfall). ()

• Still expects £450m pretax profit for the FY ending 30 April 2026, which would be -15% vs FY2025 (£528.9m), with ~£300m net cash. ()

• Profit profile: “slightly weighted” to 2027 then broadly evenly spread thereafter (per your text / market coverage). ()

• Targets reaffirmed: core-business ROCE ≥15% “as soon as possible”; 11%–15% in the interim; FY2025 ROCE was 16.5%. ()

• Targets reaffirmed: operating margin within historic 17.5%–19.5% range; aims to maintain net cash and continue reducing land creditors. ()

• Market reaction in the session: shares fell ~18% on the day (per contemporaneous reporting).
summary: Berkeley Group halts new land acquisitions and cuts investment amid economic uncertainty and geopolitical tensions

body:
Time Period Agreement announced 01 April 2026 (repurchase financing/benefits discussed through 2027+) ()

• Intel to repurchase the 49% JV equity interest in Fab 34 (Ireland) not held by Intel for $14.2bn. ()

• Implied uplift vs 2024 structure: Apollo invested $11.2bn in 2024 for the 49% stake; Intel repurchase price implies a ~26.8% premium vs that headline amount (14.2 vs 11.2). ()

• Funding: cash on hand + ~$6.5bn of new debt issuance. ()

• Intel impact: expected accretive to ongoing EPS; expected to strengthen Intel’s credit profile “in 2027 and beyond”; Intel still expects to retire maturities as due in 2026–2027. ()

• Quote (David Zinsner, Intel CFO): “We thank Apollo for their ongoing partnership… Our 2024 agreement was the right structure at the right time… Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline and an evolved business strategy…”

• Quote (Jamshid Ehsani, Apollo Partner): “Flexibility and alignment are core to how we approach relationships… we are pleased to facilitate this transaction in support of Intel’s evolving strategic and operational priorities…”

• Operational footprint note: Fab 34 described as a high-volume facility for Intel 4 / Intel 3 products (including Intel Core Ultra and Intel Xeon 6). ()
summary: Intel repurchases Apollo's 49% Fab 34 stake for $14.2bn, implying 26.8% premium

company: Concrete Pumping Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ: BBCP) / Camfaud (UK) / Templant Hire Limited
body:
Time Period Acquisition announcement (strategic entry into UK temporary power) ()

• Concrete Pumping (via Camfaud) acquired Templant Hire Limited, entering the UK temporary power market and positioning it as an adjacent-sector platform. ()

• Templant asset base: 250+ generators spanning 20 kVA to 1250 kVA, plus distribution equipment, cabling, fuel management, and on-site support. ()

• Strategic fit: intended to complement Camfaud’s UK concrete pumping footprint and broaden offering across construction/infrastructure customers. ()

• Synergy plan explicitly flagged: cross-selling, shared customer relationships, and using Camfaud’s national footprint to support Templant expansion. ()

• Financial impact framing: transaction expected to be net debt neutral. ()

• Quote (Bruce Young, President & CEO): “This acquisition represents an important step in executing our strategy to build a diversified, multi-service platform… we see clear opportunities to accelerate growth… while also delivering meaningful synergies and long-term sustainable value…”

• Quote (Tony Faud, MD, U.K. Camfaud Group): “We are delighted to be partnering with Templant… This acquisition enhances our ability to support customers with a broader range of solutions…”

• Quote (Chris Allen, MD, Templant): “Joining forces with Camfaud provides a platform to accelerate our growth, expand our geographic reach, and continue delivering high-quality solutions to our
summary: Concrete Pumping acquires Templant Hire to enter UK temporary power market via Camfaud

body:
Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1). ()

• Board expects trading and net cash in line with expectations for H1. ()

• Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted revenue £1,185.8m (~£1.19bn). ()

• Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted operating profit £77.6m. ()

• Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): pre-IFRS 16 net cash £20.0m. ()

• Implied YoY growth vs the comparative figures cited in the market write-up: adjusted revenue +109.0% (£1.19bn vs £569.3m); adjusted operating profit +131.0% (£77.6m vs £33.6m). ()

• Water: demand and momentum ahead of expectations, moving into year two of AMP8. ()

• Rail: renewals volumes lower, but offset by increased demand for maintenance (notably over winter via reactive maintenance frameworks). ()

• Infrastructure: trading resilient; positioning to capture opportunities from RIS3 commencing 01 April 2026. ()

• Energy: expansion in electricity T&D “well received”; Emerald Power (acquired Oct 2025) and Excalon performing well into H2. ()

• Civil Nuclear: Sellafield industrial action continues to weigh on performance at the site. ()

• Onshore wind: Full Circle impacted by underperformance in French subsidiary; restructuring review underway. ()

• M&A: assessing acquisitions in Environmental and Energy sectors; expects progress in H2 (active pipeline). ()

• Order book: stated as record levels. ()

• Company statement: “Underpinned by extensive Government spending commitments and long-term framework positions, the Group's order book continues to be at record levels, demonstrating our core capabilities and established presence in a diverse range of long-term, sustainable growth sectors. The Group remains strongly positioned with a well balanced portfolio of businesses, providing us with confidence in delivering against our full year expectations.” ()

• Interim results date: 12 May 2026. ()
summary: Renew Holdings reports strong H1 growth driven by water, rail and infrastructure demand

company: J Sainsbury PLC / NatWest Group PLC
body:
Time Period Partnership announcement; products launching in H2 2026. ()

• Sainsbury’s customers to access new NatWest-provided financial products (savings and loans) via Sainsbury’s digital channels. ()

• Product rollout begins in the second half of 2026. ()

• Nectar-specific proposition: “exclusive” benefits for Nectar members, including tailored rates on savings and loans. ()

• New product: NatWest Nectar credit card (earn Nectar points on everyday purchases, with bonus-point opportunities). ()

• New product: instant access savings. ()

• New product: unsecured personal loans (distributed through Sainsbury’s digital channels). ()

• Solange Chamberlain (NatWest) said the partnership combines “our expertise and financial knowhow with a seamless digital experience and Sainsbury's scale”. ()

• Patrick Short (Sainsbury’s) said: “We're committed to working with trusted partners who share our values and dedication to delivering outstanding service for our customers. Building on our success together, we are pleased to extend our agreement with NatWest, whose deep expertise and customer-first approach continue to make them an ideal partner for our evolving financial services offering.” (

• Grand Opening of “Eldorado” in Bremerton; described as LGI’s largest Seattle-area community to date with 491 homesites. ()

• VAT refunds: Ukrainian authorities’ withholding continued in Q1; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be US$80m at end-March 2026. ()

body:
Time Period Strategy update ahead of FY results (year ending 30 April 2026); guidance for FY26 and the four years to 2030. ()

• Director dealing: Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at £28.78 (c. £201,482) after an unscheduled trading update drove a sharp sell-off. ()

• FY26 guidance reiterated: expects pre-tax profit £450m (in line with guidance set two years ago) and ~£300m net cash. ()

• Capital allocation shift: will not acquire new land while current conditions prevail, except through joint ventures; focus shifts to value creation from existing land holdings. ()

• Existing land: holdings comprise 50,000+ homes, plus 10,000+ further pipeline homes (London & South-East). ()

• Cost base: targeting 25% reduction in operating costs to £150m (implies a c. £200m prior run-rate on this metric). ()

• Balance sheet / land creditors: targeting reduction to £470m from £900m (c. 48% reduction). ()

• Medium-term profit outlook: expects to deliver £1.4bn+ pre-tax profit over the next four years to 2030 (c. £350m+ p.a., ~22% lower than the FY26 £450m level on an average annual basis). ()

• Execution levers highlighted: tighter construction sequencing; flexing pace of BTR investment; disciplined capital allocation; continuing shareholder returns (with buybacks referenced as a tool when the share price is below NAV per share). ()
summary: Berkeley shifts focus to value creation from existing landbank while cutting costs and reducing debt

body:
Time Period Year ended 31 December 2025 (FY 2025); includes Q4 2025

• Pretax result swung to a USD0.2m loss (USD202k) in FY 2025 from a USD6.0m profit in FY 2024 (a negative swing of ~USD6.2m). ()

• Petroleum & natural gas sales fell 20% to USD45.8m (FY 2024: USD57.5m). ()

• FY 2025 funds flow from operations fell 68% to USD5.37m (FY 2024: USD16.75m). ()

• FY 2025 operating netback fell 35% to USD21.26m (USD12.44/boe) from USD32.89m (USD15.68/boe) — netback/boe down 21%. ()

• FY 2025 net income was USD10.89m (up 32% YoY) but was heavily driven by non-operational items (USD12.61m deferred tax recovery and USD4.98m gain on asset dispositions). ()

• Q4 2025 production averaged 4,877 boe/d, down ~7.8% YoY (Q4 2024: 5,287 boe/d). ()

• FY 2025 production averaged 4,686 boe/d, down ~18% YoY (FY 2024: 5,734 boe/d). ()

• FY 2025 net debt rose to USD72.89m (FY 2024: USD29.11m), an increase of ~150%. ()

• Liquidity / going concern: FY 2025 included a working capital deficit of USD15.4m (excluding the convertible debenture) and management projects a potential bank covenant breach by 31 Dec 2026 that could make bank debt repayable then.
summary: Touchstone's 2025 results show declining production, sales, and cash flow with rising debt concerns

• Adjusted operating margin: +120 bps to 14.8%. ()

• Dividends: total dividend 18.71p, +11% (from 16.86p); final dividend 15.27p. ()

body:
Time Period Disposal completed (announcement date 01 April 2026); asset held since December 2009; portfolio disposals measured vs March 2025 book values. ()

• Great Ropemaker Partnership (50/50 JV managed by GPE) completed the sale of the 53-year short leasehold interest in 103/113 Regent Street, W1 to a private client of JLL. ()

• Headline price: £52m, “marginally behind” March 2025 book value. ()

• Pricing metrics: 7.2% net initial yield; £912 per sq ft capital value. ()

• Asset: 56,850 sq ft, Grade II listed, Regent Street retail with offices above; fully let to UNIQLO (London flagship) until 2036. ()

• Headlease: held leasehold from The Crown Estate, expiring April 2079. ()

• Disposal roll-up: total sales in the last 12 months £490m, 2% ahead of March 2025 book values (implies ~£9.6m aggregate uplift vs book, based on the stated 2%). ()

• Investment return: acquired December 2009; sale crystallises a whole-life ungeared IRR ~10.9%. ()

• Sophie Pearce (Senior Portfolio Manager) said: “This sale marks a successful conclusion to a long-term investment for the Great Ropemaker Partnership. Since acquiring the building in 2009, we have benefited from strong occupier performance, sustained income growth and the enduring appeal of Regent Street as one of London's prime retail locations,” and “The transaction reflects our ongoing strategy to recycle capital into opportunities with greater potential for value creation, while delivering strong returns for our partners.” ()
summary: GPE sells Regent Street property for £52m, crystallising 10.9% IRR since 2009

body:
Time Period FY 2025 (year ended 31 December 2025); broker note dated 1 April 2026

• Peel Hunt downgraded CLS to ‘reduce’ (from ‘hold’) and cut price target to 45p from 60p. ()

• EPRA NTA per share fell 6.7% to 200.7p (2024: 215.0p).

• EPRA EPS fell 17.4% to 7.6p (2024: 9.2p).

• Full-year dividend cut 24% to 4.0p (2024: 5.28p).

• Net rental income fell 11.1% to £101.3m (2024: £114.0m).

• Like-for-like net rental income fell 6.3% to £106.8m (drivers cited: lease expiries, stock movements, and lower other income).

• Portfolio: like-for-like valuation decline of 3.8% (local currency basis).

• UK portfolio valuation decline of 4.6% (local currency basis).

• Leverage: balance sheet loan-to-value 50.0% (2024: 50.7%); net debt:EBITDA 12.4x (2024: 11.9x).

• Net debt fell 9.2% to £852.5m (2024: £938.7m).

• Disposals: completed £144.2m of disposals in 2025 (including Spring Mews Student at £101.1m), with management citing this helped reduce net debt by £86.2m.

• Management tone (report excerpt): “The hard work undertaken by the whole CLS team to make progress on our strategic priorities is having its desired effect…” (continues to stress near-term earnings/NTA impact but positioning for medium/long-term growth).

• Going concern disclosure: directors state a material uncertainty exists because achieving required refinancings and planned disposals is outside management’s control (despite confidence based on track record).

• Peel Hunt view (as per the broker commentary you shared): balance sheet flagged as “stretched”, with need for “significant disposals and market tailwinds” and warning that “balance sheet risk remains elevated”.
summary: CLS Holdings downgraded amid falling earnings, dividend cut and portfolio valuation declines

summary: FCA confirms motor finance redress scheme; S&U expects eligible Advantage customers to halve
body: Time Period FCA motor finance redress scheme confirmed 30 March 2026; company reaction 1 April 2026 • Welcomed the FCA’s final motor finance redress approach, saying it expects the proposals to halve the number of Advantage customers eligible for redress. () • S&U emphasised Advantage never offered discretionary commission arrangements (DCAs) (the core issue cited by the FCA). () • Karl Werner (CEO of Advantage) (full quote): “Advantage has never offered DCA commissions or engaged in tied arrangements. Our commission levels as a proportion of the charge for credit are generally well within those proposed for redress by the FCA and only exceed them for very small loans. Even before the final rules were published nearly 98% of Advantage customers did not qualify. We anticipate these finalised FCA proposals to halve the number of our customers that are eligible for redress under the scheme.” () • Werner (full quote): “Advantage also welcomed the simplification of the redress process, particularly on how customers should be contacted. The company also agreed with the FCA's proposal that the grounds for rebutting claims should include cases where there was no better deal available for the customer.” () • Werner (full quote): “Although a redress scheme of any kind is not justified for Advantage in our view, we are pleased that this matter can now be concluded by the early Autumn. The provisions we have made for its costs will prove more than adequate. It should therefore in no way impede the ambitious plans we now have in place for building Advantage.” () • FCA scheme size: regulator estimates ~12.1m agreements eligible (down ~15% from 14.2m proposed earlier). () • FCA cost estimate: ~£7.5bn compensation; ~£9.1bn total including admin (down from earlier estimates up to ~£11bn). () • Additional FCA detail from coverage: average payout cited around ~£830 per eligible agreement. ()

summary: Ferrexpo faces severe liquidity crisis with cash runway only until June 2026
body: Time Period Q1 2026 trading update (with cash position at 31 March 2026); FY2025 accounts timing update • Operations were suspended after attacks on Ukrainian energy infrastructure in Jan 2026; production restarted in Feb 2026, with one pellet line currently operating. () • Cost/working-capital actions: reduced working time, cuts to procurement, and continued suspension of all non-essential capex, overheads, and CSR spending. () • VAT refunds: withholding continued in Q1 2026; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be ~US$80m at end-March 2026. () • Liquidity: available cash US$35m at 31 Mar 2026; no debt other than lease liabilities; net cash ~US$25m (vs US$47m at 31 Dec 2025 and US$30m at 27 Feb 2026). () • Accessibility of cash: excluding funds held with MBaer Merchant Bank, net accessible cash ~US$22m at 31 Mar 2026. () • Cash runway: based on forecast cash burn, expects sufficient cash only until at least end-June 2026. () • Funding: “actively exploring” options that “could include an equity capital raise”; “no certainty” of success; warns continued VAT withholding + unresolved funding could lead to “material negative consequences”. () • FY2025 accounts: needs more time to finalise, but expects to report on or before 30 April 2026.

summary: Babcock signs six-month bridging agreement with UK MoD while negotiating new long-term FMSP deal
body: Time Period Bridging agreement effective from 1 April 2026 (following expiry of prior contract on 31 March 2026) • Signed a six-month bridging agreement with the UK Ministry of Defence under the Future Maritime Support Programme (FMSP), after the prior five-year FMSP support contract ended on 31 March 2026. () • Babcock said it is “in the latter stages of negotiation with the MoD” on a new long-term agreement. () • Company statement (full quote): “The contract bridges to a new long-term agreement which is in the latter stages of negotiation with the MOD. A Letter of Intent from the MOD sits alongside the bridging agreement and reinforces the commitment to a long-term strategic relationship between the MOD, the Royal Navy and Babcock, and the critical contribution that Babcock makes to the UK defence capability, including as the sole provider of in-service submarine support to the Royal Navy”. () • Company statement (full quote): “The future agreement supports the government and Royal Navy's priorities and aligns with the strategic defence review and defence industrial strategy.” () • Prior five-year FMSP contract was agreed in 2021 and was worth ~£3.5bn (per the company/background coverage). () • New long-term deal is expected to support “increased demand, scope and operations” at Clyde and Devonport naval bases (Clyde hosts the UK’s nuclear deterrent). () • CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “The strength of Babcock's partnership with the Ministry of Defence and Royal Navy is critical to the increased availability and resilience of the UK's submarine fleet and the defence of our nation.” () • CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “This agreement with the Submarine Delivery Agency reinforces our joint intention to move to a new long-term agreement that will see even more investment in skills, communities and infrastructure, while growing this critical, sovereign capability, together.”

summary: Derwent London sells Horseferry House to overseas investor for £131.8m in June 2026
body: Time Period Disposal announced 1 April 2026; completion scheduled for June 2026 • Agreed sale of Horseferry House, Westminster (Burberry HQ) to an “overseas investor” for £131.8m (before rental top-ups); completion expected June 2026. () • Sale price is slightly below the December (2025) book value (i.e., marginally under 31 Dec 2025 valuation). () • Derwent bought Horseferry House in 2005 for £34m; headline sale price implies ~3.9x the purchase price (c. +288% vs 2005 cost, ignoring capex/holding costs). () • Lease action (full quote): “Burberry's lease was regeared, extending the term by five years to 2043 (no breaks) and incorporating two new five-yearly fixed uplifts in 2033 and 2038”. () • Derwent has now exchanged contracts on ~£275m of sales since the start of 2026; in talks for a further ~£100m, towards its £1bn three-year disposals target. ()

summary: Compass Group shifts LSE share trading currency from GBp to USD effective April 2026
body: Time Period Change effective 1 April 2026 • LSE trading currency for Compass shares changed from GBp to USD, aligning share trading currency with Compass’s reporting currency. () • Rationale given: reduce FX-driven volatility in the share price and “simplify” the investment case for global investors. () • No change to LSE listing status or FTSE UK index eligibility. () • Dividends continue to be paid in GBP unless shareholders elect to receive them in USD. ()

summary: Saltchuk acquires Great Lakes Dredge & Dock for $1.5 billion at $17 per share
body: Time Period Transaction closed 01 April 2026; tender offer expired late 31 March 2026 () • Acquisition closed: Great Lakes became Saltchuk’s newest wholly owned subsidiary; $17.00/share cash, enterprise value ~$1.5bn. () • Quote (Mark Tabbutt, Saltchuk Chairman): “We're proud to welcome Great Lakes to the Saltchuk family of companies… With Great Lakes, we have grown to nearly 10,000 team members… We look forward to supporting Great Lakes’ reinvestment and growth ambitions for generations to come.” () • Operating model: Great Lakes to join as a stand-alone business unit, continuing to operate independently under current leadership. () • Quote (Lasse Petterson, GLDD CEO): “Joining Saltchuk’s family of companies is a proud moment for us… This partnership represents a natural alignment of values and vision…” • Quote (Petterson – strategy): “As we look ahead, we remain focused on executing our long-term growth strategy with discipline and purpose… committed to maintaining and enhancing our leadership position in U.S. dredging, the global offshore energy sector…” • Tender offer expiry: one minute after 11:59pm New York time on 31 March 2026. () • Shares tendered: 53,738,558 shares tendered and not withdrawn = 79.88% of issued/outstanding GLDD shares. () • Listing impact: before NASDAQ open on 01 April 2026, GLDD shares cease trading and then are delisted and deregistered. ()

summary: Berkeley Group halts land acquisition and cuts investment amid economic uncertainty and geopolitical concerns
body: Time Period Unscheduled trading update (FY ending 30 April 2026; outlook through FY2030) • Will stop acquiring new land and reduce work-in-progress investment to match current sales levels, citing the Iran war and deteriorating economic backdrop. () • Company framing (full quote): taking “decisive action to maximise long term shareholder value” by re-phasing delivery of the “Berkeley 2035” strategy over the four years to 2030. () • Company framing (full quote): “With the ongoing conflict and deterioration of the economic outlook, we are reducing work in progress investment to match the sales levels we are currently achieving. We are forecasting we can absorb the expected cost inflation through optimisation of our land holdings”. () • Said it won’t acquire new land while conditions prevail as it “does not believe it can make its required rate of return on investment.” () • Now guides to >£1.4bn pretax profit across FY2027–FY2030; RBC cited in your text as ~29% below Visible Alpha consensus £1.98bn (implies ~£0.58bn shortfall). () • Still expects £450m pretax profit for the FY ending 30 April 2026, which would be -15% vs FY2025 (£528.9m), with ~£300m net cash. () • Profit profile: “slightly weighted” to 2027 then broadly evenly spread thereafter (per your text / market coverage). () • Targets reaffirmed: core-business ROCE ≥15% “as soon as possible”; 11%–15% in the interim; FY2025 ROCE was 16.5%. () • Targets reaffirmed: operating margin within historic 17.5%–19.5% range; aims to maintain net cash and continue reducing land creditors. () • Market reaction in the session: shares fell ~18% on the day (per contemporaneous reporting).

summary: Intel repurchases Apollo's 49% Fab 34 stake for $14.2 billion, implying 26.8% premium
body: Time Period Agreement announced 01 April 2026 (repurchase financing/benefits discussed through 2027+) () • Intel to repurchase the 49% JV equity interest in Fab 34 (Ireland) not held by Intel for $14.2bn. () • Implied uplift vs 2024 structure: Apollo invested $11.2bn in 2024 for the 49% stake; Intel repurchase price implies a ~26.8% premium vs that headline amount (14.2 vs 11.2). () • Funding: cash on hand + ~$6.5bn of new debt issuance. () • Intel impact: expected accretive to ongoing EPS; expected to strengthen Intel’s credit profile “in 2027 and beyond”; Intel still expects to retire maturities as due in 2026–2027. () • Quote (David Zinsner, Intel CFO): “We thank Apollo for their ongoing partnership… Our 2024 agreement was the right structure at the right time… Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline and an evolved business strategy…” • Quote (Jamshid Ehsani, Apollo Partner): “Flexibility and alignment are core to how we approach relationships… we are pleased to facilitate this transaction in support of Intel’s evolving strategic and operational priorities…” • Operational footprint note: Fab 34 described as a high-volume facility for Intel 4 / Intel 3 products (including Intel Core Ultra and Intel Xeon 6). ()

summary: Concrete Pumping acquires Templant Hire to enter UK temporary power market

summary: Renew Holdings expects record order book growth with adjusted revenue and profit roughly doubling year-on-year
body: Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1). () • Board expects trading and net cash in line with expectations for H1. () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted revenue £1,185.8m (~£1.19bn). () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted operating profit £77.6m. () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): pre-IFRS 16 net cash £20.0m. () • Implied YoY growth vs the comparative figures cited in the market write-up: adjusted revenue +109.0% (£1.19bn vs £569.3m); adjusted operating profit +131.0% (£77.6m vs £33.6m). () • Water: demand and momentum ahead of expectations, moving into year two of AMP8. () • Rail: renewals volumes lower, but offset by increased demand for maintenance (notably over winter via reactive maintenance frameworks). () • Infrastructure: trading resilient; positioning to capture opportunities from RIS3 commencing 01 April 2026. () • Energy: expansion in electricity T&D “well received”; Emerald Power (acquired Oct 2025) and Excalon performing well into H2. () • Civil Nuclear: Sellafield industrial action continues to weigh on performance at the site. () • Onshore wind: Full Circle impacted by underperformance in French subsidiary; restructuring review underway. () • M&A: assessing acquisitions in Environmental and Energy sectors; expects progress in H2 (active pipeline). () • Order book: stated as record levels. () • Company statement: “Underpinned by extensive Government spending commitments and long-term framework positions, the Group's order book continues to be at record levels, demonstrating our core capabilities and established presence in a diverse range of long-term, sustainable growth sectors. The Group remains strongly positioned with a well balanced portfolio of businesses, providing us with confidence in delivering against our full year expectations.” () • Interim results date: 12 May 2026. ()

summary: NatWest and Sainsbury's to launch exclusive financial products for customers in H2 2026
body: Time Period Partnership announcement; products launching in H2 2026. Building on our success together, we are pleased to extend our agreement with NatWest, whose deep expertise and customer-first approach continue to make them an ideal partner for our evolving financial services offering.” (

summary: IG Group launches £125m share buyback programme starting April 2026
body: Time Period Buyback programme commencing 01 April 2026; first tranche expected to complete by 30 September 2026; strategic review outcome due autumn 2026. summary: LGI Homes opens Eldorado, its largest Seattle-area community with 491 homesites in Bremerton
body: Time Period Community launch / grand opening announcement (Seattle-area) () • Grand Opening of “Eldorado” in Bremerton; described as LGI’s largest Seattle-area community to date with 491 homesites. Stylish plank flooring, two-tone interior paint, and two-inch faux wood blinds throughout the home are a few more features that are all included in the sales price,” stated Penrod.
