We’re looking forward to continuing to support our active duty and veteran members yet to come,” stated Penrod. ()

summary: Ferrexpo faces severe liquidity crisis with $80m VAT refunds trapped and cash runway until June 2026
body: Time Period Q1 2026 trading/cash update (cash as at 31 March 2026); cash runway to end-June 2026; FY2025 results timing. () • VAT refunds: Ukrainian authorities’ withholding continued in Q1; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be US$80m at end-March 2026. () • Liquidity (31 Mar 2026): available cash US$35m; net cash ~US$25m (no debt except lease liabilities). () • Liquidity (31 Mar 2026): net accessible cash ~US$22m excluding funds held with MBaer Merchant Bank. () • Cash trajectory: available cash US$35m at 31 Mar 2026 vs US$47m at 31 Dec 2025 and US$30m at 27 Feb 2026. () • Scale of VAT vs liquidity: VAT refunds due (~US$80m) are ~2.3x total available cash (US$35m) and ~3.6x net accessible cash (US$22m). () • Runway: based on forecast cash burn, expects sufficient cash “until at least the end of June 2026.” () • Funding: “actively exploring” options that could include an equity capital raise; “no certainty” of success. () • Risk disclosure: “If the withholding of VAT refunds continues and funding issues are not resolved in sufficient time, this could give rise to material negative consequences for the Group”. () • Operations: suspended in January 2026 after attacks on Ukrainian energy infrastructure; restarted February 2026; one pellet line operating. () • Cost actions: reduced working time, procurement cuts, suspension of non-essential capex/overheads/CSR spend. () • Reporting: needs more time to finalise FY2025 accounts; expects to report on or before 30 April 2026. ()

summary: Berkeley Group shifts strategy to value existing land while cutting costs and reducing debt
body: Time Period Strategy update ahead of FY results (year ending 30 April 2026); guidance for FY26 and the four years to 2030. () • Director dealing: Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at £28.78 (c. £201,482) after an unscheduled trading update drove a sharp sell-off. () • FY26 guidance reiterated: expects pre-tax profit £450m (in line with guidance set two years ago) and ~£300m net cash. () • Capital allocation shift: will not acquire new land while current conditions prevail, except through joint ventures; focus shifts to value creation from existing land holdings. () • Existing land: holdings comprise 50,000+ homes, plus 10,000+ further pipeline homes (London & South-East). () • Cost base: targeting 25% reduction in operating costs to £150m (implies a c. £200m prior run-rate on this metric). () • Balance sheet / land creditors: targeting reduction to £470m from £900m (c. 48% reduction). () • Medium-term profit outlook: expects to deliver £1.4bn+ pre-tax profit over the next four years to 2030 (c. £350m+ p.a., ~22% lower than the FY26 £450m level on an average annual basis). () • Execution levers highlighted: tighter construction sequencing; flexing pace of BTR investment; disciplined capital allocation; continuing shareholder returns (with buybacks referenced as a tool when the share price is below NAV per share). ()

summary: Touchstone Exploration's 2025 results show declining production, sales, and cash flow with rising debt
body: Time Period Year ended 31 December 2025 (FY 2025); includes Q4 2025 • Pretax result swung to a USD0.2m loss (USD202k) in FY 2025 from a USD6.0m profit in FY 2024 (a negative swing of ~USD6.2m). () • Petroleum & natural gas sales fell 20% to USD45.8m (FY 2024: USD57.5m). () • FY 2025 funds flow from operations fell 68% to USD5.37m (FY 2024: USD16.75m). () • FY 2025 operating netback fell 35% to USD21.26m (USD12.44/boe) from USD32.89m (USD15.68/boe) — netback/boe down 21%. () • FY 2025 net income was USD10.89m (up 32% YoY) but was heavily driven by non-operational items (USD12.61m deferred tax recovery and USD4.98m gain on asset dispositions). () • Q4 2025 production averaged 4,877 boe/d, down ~7.8% YoY (Q4 2024: 5,287 boe/d). () • FY 2025 production averaged 4,686 boe/d, down ~18% YoY (FY 2024: 5,734 boe/d). () • FY 2025 net debt rose to USD72.89m (FY 2024: USD29.11m), an increase of ~150%. () • Liquidity / going concern: FY 2025 included a working capital deficit of USD15.4m (excluding the convertible debenture) and management projects a potential bank covenant breach by 31 Dec 2026 that could make bank debt repayable then.

summary: Stephnie Judge appointed CEO of Victorian Plumbing, replacing founder Mark Radcliffe
body: Time Period Board changes effective 1 April 2026 • Company confirmed the previously announced board changes have now taken effect. () • Stephnie Judge appointed to the Board as Chief Executive Officer, succeeding founder Mark Radcliffe. () • Mark Radcliffe moved to non-executive Founder Director; company states he will “remain actively engaged with the Group and its strategic direction” and remains a substantial shareholder. () • Additional disclosure: Stephnie Judge (age 37) holds 2,501,231 shares (~0.8%) plus options over 302,944 shares. ()

summary: AG Barr reports 18% profit growth and 11% dividend increase for FY2026
body: Time Period Financial year ended 31 January 2026; director dealing executed Tuesday 31 March 2026 (per the director-dealings report). () • Director dealing: CEO Euan Sutherland bought 11,956 shares at 662.44p (c. £79,201). () • Statutory pre-tax profit: +18% to £62.6m (from £53.2m). () • Revenue: +4.0% to £437.3m (from £420.4m). () • Basic EPS: +18% to 42.27p (from 35.81p). () • Adjusted profit before tax: £65.8m, +12.5% YoY (and marginally ahead of one cited analyst consensus point of £65.4m, i.e. ~0.6% beat). () • Adjusted operating margin: +120 bps to 14.8%. () • Dividends: total dividend 18.71p, +11% (from 16.86p); final dividend 15.27p. ()

summary: Experian enters irrevocable share repurchase agreement with Goldman Sachs through May 2026
body: Time Period Share repurchase arrangement 1 April 2026 to 19 May 2026 • Entered an irrevocable, non-discretionary arrangement with Goldman Sachs International to purchase Experian shares (ordinary shares of US$0.10 each) on Experian’s behalf between 1 Apr 2026 and 19 May 2026. () • Stated purpose: reduce shares in issue and meet obligations from employee share plans of ~US$200m. () • Company preliminary results are scheduled for 20 May 2026. () • Authority / compliance basis referenced: FCA Listing Rules (Chapter 9), Companies (Jersey) Law 1991, and shareholder authority granted at AGM on 16 July 2025. ()

summary: LGI Homes prices CNY10bn offshore notes in two tranches due 2031 and 2036
body: Time Period Notes offering priced; expected close around 10 April 2026 • Priced an offshore offering of CNY10bn senior unsecured notes (Reg S / non-US persons). () • Tranche 1: CNY7.5bn at 2.05%, due 2031. () • Tranche 2: CNY2.5bn at 2.75%, due 2036. () • Expected closing date: on or about 10 April 2026 (customary closing conditions). () • Use of proceeds: general corporate purposes, including repayment of certain existing indebtedness and payment of interest. () • Notes expected to be listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. ()

summary: GPE sells Regent Street property for £52m, achieving 10.9% IRR since 2009 acquisition
body: Time Period Disposal completed (announcement date 01 April 2026); asset held since December 2009; portfolio disposals measured vs March 2025 book values. () • Great Ropemaker Partnership (50/50 JV managed by GPE) completed the sale of the 53-year short leasehold interest in 103/113 Regent Street, W1 to a private client of JLL. () • Headline price: £52m, “marginally behind” March 2025 book value. () • Pricing metrics: 7.2% net initial yield; £912 per sq ft capital value. () • Asset: 56,850 sq ft, Grade II listed, Regent Street retail with offices above; fully let to UNIQLO (London flagship) until 2036. () • Headlease: held leasehold from The Crown Estate, expiring April 2079. () • Disposal roll-up: total sales in the last 12 months £490m, 2% ahead of March 2025 book values (implies ~£9.6m aggregate uplift vs book, based on the stated 2%). () • Investment return: acquired December 2009; sale crystallises a whole-life ungeared IRR ~10.9%. () • Sophie Pearce (Senior Portfolio Manager) said: “This sale marks a successful conclusion to a long-term investment for the Great Ropemaker Partnership. Since acquiring the building in 2009, we have benefited from strong occupier performance, sustained income growth and the enduring appeal of Regent Street as one of London's prime retail locations,” and “The transaction reflects our ongoing strategy to recycle capital into opportunities with greater potential for value creation, while delivering strong returns for our partners.” ()

summary: CLS Holdings downgraded to reduce with dividend cut and NTA decline amid portfolio weakness
body: Time Period FY 2025 (year ended 31 December 2025); broker note dated 1 April 2026 • Peel Hunt downgraded CLS to ‘reduce’ (from ‘hold’) and cut price target to 45p from 60p. () • EPRA NTA per share fell 6.7% to 200.7p (2024: 215.0p). • EPRA EPS fell 17.4% to 7.6p (2024: 9.2p). • Full-year dividend cut 24% to 4.0p (2024: 5.28p). • Net rental income fell 11.1% to £101.3m (2024: £114.0m). • Like-for-like net rental income fell 6.3% to £106.8m (drivers cited: lease expiries, stock movements, and lower other income). • Portfolio: like-for-like valuation decline of 3.8% (local currency basis). • UK portfolio valuation decline of 4.6% (local currency basis). • Leverage: balance sheet loan-to-value 50.0% (2024: 50.7%); net debt:EBITDA 12.4x (2024: 11.9x). • Net debt fell 9.2% to £852.5m (2024: £938.7m). • Disposals: completed £144.2m of disposals in 2025 (including Spring Mews Student at £101.1m), with management citing this helped reduce net debt by £86.2m. • Management tone (report excerpt): “The hard work undertaken by the whole CLS team to make progress on our strategic priorities is having its desired effect…” (continues to stress near-term earnings/NTA impact but positioning for medium/long-term growth). • Going concern disclosure: directors state a material uncertainty exists because achieving required refinancings and planned disposals is outside management’s control (despite confidence based on track record). • Peel Hunt view (as per the broker commentary you shared): balance sheet flagged as “stretched”, with need for “significant disposals and market tailwinds” and warning that “balance sheet risk remains elevated”.

summary: Pamela Bingham becomes CEO of Alumasc Group PLC on 1 April 2026
body: Time Period Executive change effective 1 April 2026 • Pamela Bingham (previously CEO Designate) has been appointed Chief Executive Officer with effect from 1 April 2026. () • Bingham joined the Board as CEO Designate on 2 March 2026. () • Appointment follows Paul Hooper’s retirement on 31 March 2026. ()

summary: Topps Tiles reports flat revenue but outperforms market while closing underperforming stores
body: Time Period H1 FY2026: 26 weeks to 28 March 2026 • Plans to close 23 underperforming stores over the financial year as part of cost reduction to improve profitability (acknowledges this will reduce revenue). () • Group revenue (incl. CTD) £142.7m, -0.1% YoY in the 26 weeks to 28 March 2026. () • Said sales were hurt by volume loss from the “lengthy” Competition & Markets Authority process. () • Topps Tiles brand LFL revenue +0.1% YoY (H1). () • Ex-CTD revenue +2.1% YoY (H1). () • Q2 trading: after a “robust” Q1, revenue growth “moderated slightly” but remained positive at +0.6% (Q2). () • CTD: acquired Aug 2024; currently trading from 22 stores vs 31 a year earlier (impacted by property decisions + required disposal of four stores following CMA investigation). () • CTD performance: on track to return to profit in the FY; CTD LFL growth +1.0% in H1. () • Market comparison: says it outperformed the wider UK DIY market, which it cited as down ~2.5% over the equivalent period (Barclays UK Consumer Spend Report category data referenced). () • Online brands sales +21% in H1. () • Fired Earth: traded “well” since acquisition; delivered a positive profit in H1, with further growth expected in H2; acquisition completed “last December” (Dec 2025). ()

summary: Wix repurchased 17.6 million shares at $92 each for $1.62 billion
body:
• Announced final results of its “modified Dutch Auction” tender offer, which expired “at one (1) minute after 11:59 P.M., New York City time, on April 1, 2026.”
  • Deposit count (Equiniti Trust Company, LLC): 17,577,250 ordinary shares were properly tendered and not properly withdrawn at or below $92.00 per share.
  • Notes that not all shares tendered via notice of guaranteed delivery were delivered within the required settlement period.
  • Accepted for payment 17,577,250 shares (all properly tendered and not properly withdrawn) at $92.00 per share.
  • Aggregate repurchase cost stated as ~$1.617 billion, excluding fees and expenses.
  • Repurchased shares represent ~29.7% of shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026.
  • Implied total shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026 are ~59.2 million (based on 17.577m being 29.7%).
  • Nir Zohar, President and Co-founder, stated: “I am very pleased with the outcome of our Dutch auction tender offer, which is enabling us to repurchase nearly 30% of our market capitalization and return meaningful value to investors. We believe the results of this transaction reflect shared conviction among our broader shareholder base in our long-term strateg”

summary: FCA motor finance redress scheme finalised; S&U expects Advantage customer eligibility to halve
body: Time Period FCA motor finance redress scheme confirmed 30 March 2026; company reaction 1 April 2026 • Welcomed the FCA’s final motor finance redress approach, saying it expects the proposals to halve the number of Advantage customers eligible for redress. () • S&U emphasised Advantage never offered discretionary commission arrangements (DCAs) (the core issue cited by the FCA). () • Karl Werner (CEO of Advantage) (full quote): “Advantage has never offered DCA commissions or engaged in tied arrangements. Our commission levels as a proportion of the charge for credit are generally well within those proposed for redress by the FCA and only exceed them for very small loans. Even before the final rules were published nearly 98% of Advantage customers did not qualify. We anticipate these finalised FCA proposals to halve the number of our customers that are eligible for redress under the scheme.” () • Werner (full quote): “Advantage also welcomed the simplification of the redress process, particularly on how customers should be contacted. The company also agreed with the FCA's proposal that the grounds for rebutting claims should include cases where there was no better deal available for the customer.” () • Werner (full quote): “Although a redress scheme of any kind is not justified for Advantage in our view, we are pleased that this matter can now be concluded by the early Autumn. The provisions we have made for its costs will prove more than adequate. It should therefore in no way impede the ambitious plans we now have in place for building Advantage.” () • FCA scheme size: regulator estimates ~12.1m agreements eligible (down ~15% from 14.2m proposed earlier). () • FCA cost estimate: ~£7.5bn compensation; ~£9.1bn total including admin (down from earlier estimates up to ~£11bn). () • Additional FCA detail from coverage: average payout cited around ~£830 per eligible agreement. ()

summary: Ferrexpo faces severe liquidity crisis with cash runway until June 2026 and VAT refund withholding
body: Time Period Q1 2026 trading update (with cash position at 31 March 2026); FY2025 accounts timing update • Operations were suspended after attacks on Ukrainian energy infrastructure in Jan 2026; production restarted in Feb 2026, with one pellet line currently operating. () • Cost/working-capital actions: reduced working time, cuts to procurement, and continued suspension of all non-essential capex, overheads, and CSR spending. () • VAT refunds: withholding continued in Q1 2026; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be ~US$80m at end-March 2026. () • Liquidity: available cash US$35m at 31 Mar 2026; no debt other than lease liabilities; net cash ~US$25m (vs US$47m at 31 Dec 2025 and US$30m at 27 Feb 2026). () • Accessibility of cash: excluding funds held with MBaer Merchant Bank, net accessible cash ~US$22m at 31 Mar 2026. () • Cash runway: based on forecast cash burn, expects sufficient cash only until at least end-June 2026. () • Funding: “actively exploring” options that “could include an equity capital raise”; “no certainty” of success; warns continued VAT withholding + unresolved funding could lead to “material negative consequences”. () • FY2025 accounts: needs more time to finalise, but expects to report on or before 30 April 2026.

summary: Babcock signs six-month bridging agreement with UK Ministry of Defence on FMSP contract
body: Time Period Bridging agreement effective from 1 April 2026 (following expiry of prior contract on 31 March 2026) • Signed a six-month bridging agreement with the UK Ministry of Defence under the Future Maritime Support Programme (FMSP), after the prior five-year FMSP support contract ended on 31 March 2026. () • Babcock said it is “in the latter stages of negotiation with the MoD” on a new long-term agreement. () • Company statement (full quote): “The contract bridges to a new long-term agreement which is in the latter stages of negotiation with the MOD. A Letter of Intent from the MOD sits alongside the bridging agreement and reinforces the commitment to a long-term strategic relationship between the MOD, the Royal Navy and Babcock, and the critical contribution that Babcock makes to the UK defence capability, including as the sole provider of in-service submarine support to the Royal Navy”. () • Company statement (full quote): “The future agreement supports the government and Royal Navy's priorities and aligns with the strategic defence review and defence industrial strategy.” () • Prior five-year FMSP contract was agreed in 2021 and was worth ~£3.5bn (per the company/background coverage). () • New long-term deal is expected to support “increased demand, scope and operations” at Clyde and Devonport naval bases (Clyde hosts the UK’s nuclear deterrent). () • CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “The strength of Babcock's partnership with the Ministry of Defence and Royal Navy is critical to the increased availability and resilience of the UK's submarine fleet and the defence of our nation.” () • CEO David Lockwood (full quote): “This agreement with the Submarine Delivery Agency reinforces our joint intention to move to a new long-term agreement that will see even more investment in skills, communities and infrastructure, while growing this critical, sovereign capability, together.”

summary: Derwent London sells Horseferry House to overseas investor for £131.8m in June 2026
body: Time Period Disposal announced 1 April 2026; completion scheduled for June 2026 • Agreed sale of Horseferry House, Westminster (Burberry HQ) to an “overseas investor” for £131.8m (before rental top-ups); completion expected June 2026. () • Sale price is slightly below the December (2025) book value (i.e., marginally under 31 Dec 2025 valuation). () • Derwent bought Horseferry House in 2005 for £34m; headline sale price implies ~3.9x the purchase price (c. +288% vs 2005 cost, ignoring capex/holding costs). () • Lease action (full quote): “Burberry's lease was regeared, extending the term by five years to 2043 (no breaks) and incorporating two new five-yearly fixed uplifts in 2033 and 2038”. () • Derwent has now exchanged contracts on ~£275m of sales since the start of 2026; in talks for a further ~£100m, towards its £1bn three-year disposals target. ()

summary: Compass Group shifts LSE share trading currency from GBp to USD from April 2026
body: Time Period Change effective 1 April 2026 • LSE trading currency for Compass shares changed from GBp to USD, aligning share trading currency with Compass’s reporting currency. () • Rationale given: reduce FX-driven volatility in the share price and “simplify” the investment case for global investors. () • No change to LSE listing status or FTSE UK index eligibility. () • Dividends continue to be paid in GBP unless shareholders elect to receive them in USD. ()

summary: Saltchuk acquires Great Lakes Dredge & Dock for $1.5 billion at $17 per share
body: Time Period Transaction closed 01 April 2026; tender offer expired late 31 March 2026 () • Acquisition closed: Great Lakes became Saltchuk’s newest wholly owned subsidiary; $17.00/share cash, enterprise value ~$1.5bn. () • Quote (Mark Tabbutt, Saltchuk Chairman): “We're proud to welcome Great Lakes to the Saltchuk family of companies… With Great Lakes, we have grown to nearly 10,000 team members… We look forward to supporting Great Lakes’ reinvestment and growth ambitions for generations to come.” () • Operating model: Great Lakes to join as a stand-alone business unit, continuing to operate independently under current leadership. () • Quote (Lasse Petterson, GLDD CEO): “Joining Saltchuk’s family of companies is a proud moment for us… This partnership represents a natural alignment of values and vision…” • Quote (Petterson – strategy): “As we look ahead, we remain focused on executing our long-term growth strategy with discipline and purpose… committed to maintaining and enhancing our leadership position in U.S. dredging, the global offshore energy sector…” • Tender offer expiry: one minute after 11:59pm New York time on 31 March 2026. () • Shares tendered: 53,738,558 shares tendered and not withdrawn = 79.88% of issued/outstanding GLDD shares. () • Listing impact: before NASDAQ open on 01 April 2026, GLDD shares cease trading and then are delisted and deregistered. ()

summary: Berkeley Group halts land acquisitions and cuts investment amid economic uncertainty and geopolitical risks
body: Time Period Unscheduled trading update (FY ending 30 April 2026; outlook through FY2030) • Will stop acquiring new land and reduce work-in-progress investment to match current sales levels, citing the Iran war and deteriorating economic backdrop. () • Company framing (full quote): taking “decisive action to maximise long term shareholder value” by re-phasing delivery of the “Berkeley 2035” strategy over the four years to 2030. () • Company framing (full quote): “With the ongoing conflict and deterioration of the economic outlook, we are reducing work in progress investment to match the sales levels we are currently achieving. We are forecasting we can absorb the expected cost inflation through optimisation of our land holdings”. () • Said it won’t acquire new land while conditions prevail as it “does not believe it can make its required rate of return on investment.” () • Now guides to >£1.4bn pretax profit across FY2027–FY2030; RBC cited in your text as ~29% below Visible Alpha consensus £1.98bn (implies ~£0.58bn shortfall). () • Still expects £450m pretax profit for the FY ending 30 April 2026, which would be -15% vs FY2025 (£528.9m), with ~£300m net cash. () • Profit profile: “slightly weighted” to 2027 then broadly evenly spread thereafter (per your text / market coverage). () • Targets reaffirmed: core-business ROCE ≥15% “as soon as possible”; 11%–15% in the interim; FY2025 ROCE was 16.5%. () • Targets reaffirmed: operating margin within historic 17.5%–19.5% range; aims to maintain net cash and continue reducing land creditors. () • Market reaction in the session: shares fell ~18% on the day (per contemporaneous reporting).

summary: Intel repurchases Apollo's 49% Fab 34 stake for $14.2bn at 26.8% premium
body: Time Period Agreement announced 01 April 2026 (repurchase financing/benefits discussed through 2027+) () • Intel to repurchase the 49% JV equity interest in Fab 34 (Ireland) not held by Intel for $14.2bn. () • Implied uplift vs 2024 structure: Apollo invested $11.2bn in 2024 for the 49% stake; Intel repurchase price implies a ~26.8% premium vs that headline amount (14.2 vs 11.2). () • Funding: cash on hand + ~$6.5bn of new debt issuance. () • Intel impact: expected accretive to ongoing EPS; expected to strengthen Intel’s credit profile “in 2027 and beyond”; Intel still expects to retire maturities as due in 2026–2027. () • Quote (David Zinsner, Intel CFO): “We thank Apollo for their ongoing partnership… Our 2024 agreement was the right structure at the right time… Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline and an evolved business strategy…” • Quote (Jamshid Ehsani, Apollo Partner): “Flexibility and alignment are core to how we approach relationships… we are pleased to facilitate this transaction in support of Intel’s evolving strategic and operational priorities…” • Operational footprint note: Fab 34 described as a high-volume facility for Intel 4 / Intel 3 products (including Intel Core Ultra and Intel Xeon 6). ()

summary: Concrete Pumping acquires UK temporary power company Templant to diversify service offerings
body: Time Period Acquisition announcement (strategic entry into UK temporary power) () • Concrete Pumping (via Camfaud) acquired Templant Hire Limited, entering the UK temporary power market and positioning it as an adjacent-sector platform. () • Templant asset base: 250+ generators spanning 20 kVA to 1250 kVA, plus distribution equipment, cabling, fuel management, and on-site support. () • Strategic fit: intended to complement Camfaud’s UK concrete pumping footprint and broaden offering across construction/infrastructure customers. () • Synergy plan explicitly flagged: cross-selling, shared customer relationships, and using Camfaud’s national footprint to support Templant expansion. () • Financial impact framing: transaction expected to be net debt neutral. () • Quote (Bruce Young, President & CEO): “This acquisition represents an important step in executing our strategy to build a diversified, multi-service platform… we see clear opportunities to accelerate growth… while also delivering meaningful synergies and long-term sustainable value…” • Quote (Tony Faud, MD, U.K. Camfaud Group): “We are delighted to be partnering with Templant… This acquisition enhances our ability to support customers with a broader range of solutions…” • Quote (Chris Allen, MD, Templant): “Joining forces with Camfaud provides a platform to accelerate our growth, expand our geographic reach, and continue delivering high-quality solutions to our

summary: Renew Holdings reports strong H1 growth across most divisions with record order book
body: Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1). () • Board expects trading and net cash in line with expectations for H1. () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted revenue £1,185.8m (~£1.19bn). () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): adjusted operating profit £77.6m. () • Company-compiled analyst consensus (H1): pre-IFRS 16 net cash £20.0m. () • Implied YoY growth vs the comparative figures cited in the market write-up: adjusted revenue +109.0% (£1.19bn vs £569.3m); adjusted operating profit +131.0% (£77.6m vs £33.6m). () • Water: demand and momentum ahead of expectations, moving into year two of AMP8. () • Rail: renewals volumes lower, but offset by increased demand for maintenance (notably over winter via reactive maintenance frameworks). () • Infrastructure: trading resilient; positioning to capture opportunities from RIS3 commencing 01 April 2026. () • Energy: expansion in electricity T&D “well received”; Emerald Power (acquired Oct 2025) and Excalon performing well into H2. () • Civil Nuclear: Sellafield industrial action continues to weigh on performance at the site. () • Onshore wind: Full Circle impacted by underperformance in French subsidiary; restructuring review underway. () • M&A: assessing acquisitions in Environmental and Energy sectors; expects progress in H2 (active pipeline). () • Order book: stated as record levels. () • Company statement: “Underpinned by extensive Government spending commitments and long-term framework positions, the Group's order book continues to be at record levels, demonstrating our core capabilities and established presence in a diverse range of long-term, sustainable growth sectors. The Group remains strongly positioned with a well balanced portfolio of businesses, providing us with confidence in delivering against our full year expectations.” () • Interim results date: 12 May 2026. ()

summary: NatWest to launch savings, loans and credit card products through Sainsbury's in H2 2026
body: Time Period Partnership announcement; products launching in H2 2026. () • Sainsbury’s customers to access new NatWest-provided financial products (savings and loans) via Sainsbury’s digital channels. () • Product rollout begins in the second half of 2026. () • Nectar-specific proposition: “exclusive” benefits for Nectar members, including tailored rates on savings and loans. () • New product: NatWest Nectar credit card (earn Nectar points on everyday purchases, with bonus-point opportunities). () • New product: instant access savings. () • New product: unsecured personal loans (distributed through Sainsbury’s digital channels). () • Strategic context: follows NatWest’s 2025 acquisition of Sainsbury’s Bank’s core banking business (personal loans, credit cards, retail deposits). () • Solange Chamberlain (NatWest) said the partnership combines “our expertise and financial knowhow with a seamless digital experience and Sainsbury's scale”. () • Patrick Short (Sainsbury’s) said: “We're committed to working with trusted partners who share our values and dedication to delivering outstanding service for our customers. Building on our success together, we are pleased to extend our agreement with NatWest, whose deep expertise and customer-first approach continue to make them an ideal partner for our evolving financial services offering.” (

summary: IG Group launches £125m share buyback programme starting April 2026
body: Time Period Buyback programme commencing 01 April 2026; first tranche expected to complete by 30 September 2026; strategic review outcome due autumn 2026. () • Commenced new share buyback programme totalling up to £125m. () • Tranche structure: first tranche up to £62.5m (50% of total programme), running 01 Apr 2026 – 30 Sep 2026; second tranche to be announced separately. () • Execution: appointed Morgan Stanley to conduct the programme under pre-set parameters. () • Stated purpose: reduce share capital (shares to be held in treasury per the RNS wording). () • Buyback capacity: up to 36,155,787 shares available for repurchase under the relevant AGM authority referenced in the RNS. () • Strategic review (announced 19 Mar 2026) to consider value-maximisation options including acquisitions, domicile/listing venues, and possible business combinations; outcome due autumn 2026. () • Governance: FCA-approved Chair appointment means Andrew Barron assumes the role from 01 April 2026. ()

summary: LGI Homes opens Eldorado, its largest Seattle-area community with 491 homesites in Bremerton
body: Time Period Community launch / grand opening announcement (Seattle-area) () • Grand Opening of “Eldorado” in Bremerton; described as LGI’s largest Seattle-area community to date with 491 homesites. () • Location positioning: Kitsap Peninsula, with access to Downtown Bremerton and Silverdale. () • Quote (Zachary Penrod, VP Sales): “Eldorado is perfectly positioned for families, located just five minutes from Silverdale Waterfront Park and ten minutes from everything that Silverdale has to offer,” stated Zachary Penrod, Vice President of Sales. () • Amenities: “more than ten acres of amenities,” valued at ~$3 million (walking trails, playground, recreational fields, picnic areas). () • Product/pricing: 3/4/5-bedroom homes “starting in the low $500s.” () • Quote (Penrod on spec/package): “We are so excited to offer our CompleteHome Plus™ package which includes desirable features like energy-efficient, stainless-steel Whirlpool® kitchen appliances, stunning quartz countertops, 42” upper cabinets, and recessed ENERGY STAR lighting. Stylish plank flooring, two-tone interior paint, and two-inch faux wood blinds throughout the home are a few more features that are all included in the sales price,” stated Penrod. () • Quote (Penrod on military): “Thank you to all of the military members that we have met so far from the multiple bases close to Eldorado. We’re looking forward to continuing to support our active duty and veteran members yet to come,” stated Penrod. ()

summary: Ferrexpo faces severe liquidity crisis with only US$35m cash against US$80m VAT refunds withheld
body: Time Period Q1 2026 trading/cash update (cash as at 31 March 2026); cash runway to end-June 2026; FY2025 results timing. () • VAT refunds: Ukrainian authorities’ withholding continued in Q1; outstanding VAT refunds expected to be US$80m at end-March 2026. () • Liquidity (31 Mar 2026): available cash US$35m; net cash ~US$25m (no debt except lease liabilities). () • Liquidity (31 Mar 2026): net accessible cash ~US$22m excluding funds held with MBaer Merchant Bank. () • Cash trajectory: available cash US$35m at 31 Mar 2026 vs US$47m at 31 Dec 2025 and US$30m at 27 Feb 2026. () • Scale of VAT vs liquidity: VAT refunds due (~US$80m) are ~2.3x total available cash (US$35m) and ~3.6x net accessible cash (US$22m). () • Runway: based on forecast cash burn, expects sufficient cash “until at least the end of June 2026.” () • Funding: “actively exploring” options that could include an equity capital raise; “no certainty” of success. () • Risk disclosure: “If the withholding of VAT refunds continues and funding issues are not resolved in sufficient time, this could give rise to material negative consequences for the Group”. () • Operations: suspended in January 2026 after attacks on Ukrainian energy infrastructure; restarted February 2026; one pellet line operating. () • Cost actions: reduced working time, procurement cuts, suspension of non-essential capex/overheads/CSR spend. () • Reporting: needs more time to finalise FY2025 accounts; expects to report on or before 30 April 2026. ()

summary: Berkeley pauses land acquisition, targets cost cuts and debt reduction amid market slowdown
body: Time Period Strategy update ahead of FY results (year ending 30 April 2026); guidance for FY26 and the four years to 2030. () • Director dealing: Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at £28.78 (c. £201,482) after an unscheduled trading update drove a sharp sell-off. () • FY26 guidance reiterated: expects pre-tax profit £450m (in line with guidance set two years ago) and ~£300m net cash. () • Capital allocation shift: will not acquire new land while current conditions prevail, except through joint ventures; focus shifts to value creation from existing land holdings. () • Existing land: holdings comprise 50,000+ homes, plus 10,000+ further pipeline homes (London & South-East). () • Cost base: targeting 25% reduction in operating costs to £150m (implies a c. £200m prior run-rate on this metric). () • Balance sheet / land creditors: targeting reduction to £470m from £900m (c. 48% reduction). () • Medium-term profit outlook: expects to deliver £1.4bn+ pre-tax profit over the next four years to 2030 (c. £350m+ p.a., ~22% lower than the FY26 £450m level on an average annual basis). () • Execution levers highlighted: tighter construction sequencing; flexing pace of BTR investment; disciplined capital allocation; continuing shareholder returns (with buybacks referenced as a tool when the share price is below NAV per share). ()

summary: Touchstone Exploration's 2025 results deteriorated significantly with production down, debt surging, and going concern risks emerging
body: Time Period Year ended 31 December 2025 (FY 2025); includes Q4 2025 • Pretax result swung to a USD0.2m loss (USD202k) in FY 2025 from a USD6.0m profit in FY 2024 (a negative swing of ~USD6.2m). () • Petroleum & natural gas sales fell 20% to USD45.8m (FY 2024: USD57.5m). () • FY 2025 funds flow from operations fell 68% to USD5.37m (FY 2024: USD16.75m). () • FY 2025 operating netback fell 35% to USD21.26m (USD12.44/boe) from USD32.89m (USD15.68/boe) — netback/boe down 21%. () • FY 2025 net income was USD10.89m (up 32% YoY) but was heavily driven by non-operational items (USD12.61m deferred tax recovery and USD4.98m gain on asset dispositions). () • Q4 2025 production averaged 4,877 boe/d, down ~7.8% YoY (Q4 2024: 5,287 boe/d). () • FY 2025 production averaged 4,686 boe/d, down ~18% YoY (FY 2024: 5,734 boe/d). () • FY 2025 net debt rose to USD72.89m (FY 2024: USD29.11m), an increase of ~150%. () • Liquidity / going concern: FY 2025 included a working capital deficit of USD15.4m (excluding the convertible debenture) and management projects a potential bank covenant breach by 31 Dec 2026 that could make bank debt repayable then.

summary: Stephnie Judge appointed CEO of Victorian Plumbing, replacing founder Mark Radcliffe
body: Time Period Board changes effective 1 April 2026 • Company confirmed the previously announced board changes have now taken effect. () • Stephnie Judge appointed to the Board as Chief Executive Officer, succeeding founder Mark Radcliffe. () • Mark Radcliffe moved to non-executive Founder Director; company states he will “remain actively engaged with the Group and its strategic direction” and remains a substantial shareholder. () • Additional disclosure: Stephnie Judge (age 37) holds 2,501,231 shares (~0.8%) plus options over 302,944 shares. ()

summary: AG Barr reports 18% profit growth and 11% dividend increase for FY2026
body: Time Period Financial year ended 31 January 2026; director dealing executed Tuesday 31 March 2026 (per the director-dealings report). () • Director dealing: CEO Euan Sutherland bought 11,956 shares at 662.44p (c. £79,201). () • Statutory pre-tax profit: +18% to £62.6m (from £53.2m). () • Revenue: +4.0% to £437.3m (from £420.4m). () • Basic EPS: +18% to 42.27p (from 35.81p). () • Adjusted profit before tax: £65.8m, +12.5% YoY (and marginally ahead of one cited analyst consensus point of £65.4m, i.e. ~0.6% beat). () • Adjusted operating margin: +120 bps to 14.8%. () • Dividends: total dividend 18.71p, +11% (from 16.86p); final dividend 15.27p. ()

summary: Experian enters irrevocable share repurchase agreement with Goldman Sachs for approximately US$200m
body: Time Period Share repurchase arrangement 1 April 2026 to 19 May 2026 • Entered an irrevocable, non-discretionary arrangement with Goldman Sachs International to purchase Experian shares (ordinary shares of US$0.10 each) on Experian’s behalf between 1 Apr 2026 and 19 May 2026. () • Stated purpose: reduce shares in issue and meet obligations from employee share plans of ~US$200m. () • Company preliminary results are scheduled for 20 May 2026. () • Authority / compliance basis referenced: FCA Listing Rules (Chapter 9), Companies (Jersey) Law 1991, and shareholder authority granted at AGM on 16 July 2025. ()

summary: LGI Homes prices CNY10bn offshore senior unsecured notes in two tranches closing April 2026
body: Time Period Notes offering priced; expected close around 10 April 2026 • Priced an offshore offering of CNY10bn senior unsecured notes (Reg S / non-US persons). () • Tranche 1: CNY7.5bn at 2.05%, due 2031. () • Tranche 2: CNY2.5bn at 2.75%, due 2036. () • Expected closing date: on or about 10 April 2026 (customary closing conditions). () • Use of proceeds: general corporate purposes, including repayment of certain existing indebtedness and payment of interest. () • Notes expected to be listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. ()

summary: GPE completes £52m Regent Street sale, achieving 10.9% IRR since 2009
body: Time Period Disposal completed (announcement date 01 April 2026); asset held since December 2009; portfolio disposals measured vs March 2025 book values. () • Great Ropemaker Partnership (50/50 JV managed by GPE) completed the sale of the 53-year short leasehold interest in 103/113 Regent Street, W1 to a private client of JLL. () • Headline price: £52m, “marginally behind” March 2025 book value. () • Pricing metrics: 7.2% net initial yield; £912 per sq ft capital value. () • Asset: 56,850 sq ft, Grade II listed, Regent Street retail with offices above; fully let to UNIQLO (London flagship) until 2036. () • Headlease: held leasehold from The Crown Estate, expiring April 2079. () • Disposal roll-up: total sales in the last 12 months £490m, 2% ahead of March 2025 book values (implies ~£9.6m aggregate uplift vs book, based on the stated 2%). () • Investment return: acquired December 2009; sale crystallises a whole-life ungeared IRR ~10.9%. () • Sophie Pearce (Senior Portfolio Manager) said: “This sale marks a successful conclusion to a long-term investment for the Great Ropemaker Partnership. Since acquiring the building in 2009, we have benefited from strong occupier performance, sustained income growth and the enduring appeal of Regent Street as one of London's prime retail locations,” and “The transaction reflects our ongoing strategy to recycle capital into opportunities with greater potential for value creation, while delivering strong returns for our partners.” ()

summary: CLS Holdings downgraded amid falling earnings, NTA and dividend cut to 4.0p
body: Time Period FY 2025 (year ended 31 December 2025); broker note dated 1 April 2026 • Peel Hunt downgraded CLS to ‘reduce’ (from ‘hold’) and cut price target to 45p from 60p. () • EPRA NTA per share fell 6.7% to 200.7p (2024: 215.0p). • EPRA EPS fell 17.4% to 7.6p (2024: 9.2p). • Full-year dividend cut 24% to 4.0p (2024: 5.28p). • Net rental income fell 11.1% to £101.3m (2024: £114.0m). • Like-for-like net rental income fell 6.3% to £106.8m (drivers cited: lease expiries, stock movements, and lower other income). • Portfolio: like-for-like valuation decline of 3.8% (local currency basis). • UK portfolio valuation decline of 4.6% (local currency basis). • Leverage: balance sheet loan-to-value 50.0% (2024: 50.7%); net debt:EBITDA 12.4x (2024: 11.9x). • Net debt fell 9.2% to £852.5m (2024: £938.7m). • Disposals: completed £144.2m of disposals in 2025 (including Spring Mews Student at £101.1m), with management citing this helped reduce net debt by £86.2m. • Management tone (report excerpt): “The hard work undertaken by the whole CLS team to make progress on our strategic priorities is having its desired effect…” (continues to stress near-term earnings/NTA impact but positioning for medium/long-term growth). • Going concern disclosure: directors state a material uncertainty exists because achieving required refinancings and planned disposals is outside management’s control (despite confidence based on track record). • Peel Hunt view (as per the broker commentary you shared): balance sheet flagged as “stretched”, with need for “significant disposals and market tailwinds” and warning that “balance sheet risk remains elevated”.

summary: Pamela Bingham becomes CEO of Alumasc Group PLC on 1 April 2026
body: Time Period Executive change effective 1 April 2026 • Pamela Bingham (previously CEO Designate) has been appointed Chief Executive Officer with effect from 1 April 2026. () • Bingham joined the Board as CEO Designate on 2 March 2026. () • Appointment follows Paul Hooper’s retirement on 31 March 2026. ()

summary: Topps Tiles reports flat revenue but outperforms market amid store closures and CMA challenges
body: Time Period H1 FY2026: 26 weeks to 28 March 2026 • Plans to close 23 underperforming stores over the financial year as part of cost reduction to improve profitability (acknowledges this will reduce revenue). () • Group revenue (incl. CTD) £142.7m, -0.1% YoY in the 26 weeks to 28 March 2026. () • Said sales were hurt by volume loss from the “lengthy” Competition & Markets Authority process. () • Topps Tiles brand LFL revenue +0.1% YoY (H1). () • Ex-CTD revenue +2.1% YoY (H1). () • Q2 trading: after a “robust” Q1, revenue growth “moderated slightly” but remained positive at +0.6% (Q2). () • CTD: acquired Aug 2024; currently trading from 22 stores vs 31 a year earlier (impacted by property decisions + required disposal of four stores following CMA investigation). () • CTD performance: on track to return to profit in the FY; CTD LFL growth +1.0% in H1. () • Market comparison: says it outperformed the wider UK DIY market, which it cited as down ~2.5% over the equivalent period (Barclays UK Consumer Spend Report category data referenced). () • Online brands sales +21% in H1. () • Fired Earth: traded “well” since acquisition; delivered a positive profit in H1, with further growth expected in H2; acquisition completed “last December” (Dec 2025). ()

• Dealing price: £93.65 per share. ()

• Gross proceeds: £93,646.70. ()
summary: Giorgi Gureshidze sold 1,000 Lion Finance shares for £93.65 each on March 26

body:
Time Period Deal announced 31 March 2026; targeted completion mid-2027 ()

• Unilever confirmed it will combine its Foods business with McCormick; Unilever Foods implied enterprise value $44.8bn. ()

• Consideration mix: Unilever + Unilever shareholders receive McCormick stock equating to 65.0% of fully diluted combined company equity (split: Unilever shareholders 55.1%, Unilever itself 9.9%) plus $15.7bn cash. ()

• Unilever intends to sell down its retained 9.9% stake “not earlier than one year after closing” (lock-up period). ()

• Valuation metrics disclosed by Unilever: EV/Sales 3.6x and EV/EBITDA 13.8x (based on McCormick 1-month VWAP referenced in the release). ()

• Synergies: combined company expects ~$600m annual run-rate cost synergies (net of growth reinvestments) with “full value” targeted by end of year three; incremental cost/revenue synergies of $100m to be reinvested. ()

• Cash usage framework (per Unilever): $15.7bn cash intended to offset one-off separation/tax costs, reduce leverage back to ~2.0x net debt/EBITDA post-close, and support €6bn share buybacks (2026–2029). ()

• Unilever disclosed €400–500m stranded costs from separating Foods, plus €500m one-off restructuring costs (2027–2029) allocated to offset stranded costs; TSA expected for ~2 years post-close. ()

• Market reaction described in your note: Unilever shares fell 7.3% on Tuesday and a further 2.1% on Wednesday (cumulative ≈ 9.2% over the two moves); market cap quoted at £89.93bn at 4,109p.

• Barclays quote: “Despite the reaction, we like the deal structure. The 13.8x Ebitda multiple and cash component was better than expected and the stranded overheads outlined lower than expected. We liked the clarity on no transformational acquisitions. The prize of a pure play HPC [home and personal care] company will be worth it in the end.”

• Barclays quote (concerns): “There seems to be unease regarding the complexity of the deal structure and particularly the equity component of the consideration,” Barclays said.

• UBS quote (rating / transition risk): UBS reiterated ‘sell’ and said the deal leaves investors facing “a complex and uncertain 15 months ahead”.

• UBS view (financial shape): UBS said it should improve underlying sales/volume growth, but reduce reported sales and operating profit by ~20% (roughly “a fifth”) and slightly dilute operating margins.

• JPMorgan quote: “From a strategic standpoint, both deals make strong sense,” JPMorgan said…

• JPMorgan quote (value creation hinge): “The bridge to value creation from the deal hinges on re-rating of the core HPC company,” JPMorgan said…

• Barclays quote (strategy framing): “We don't subscribe to the 'desperation' narrative and think the move is a elegant solution,” the bank said, describing it as “a part of Unilever's long term strategy pivot.”

• Barclays disclosure: remains overweight with 5,500p price target (implying ~31% upside vs the Tuesday close cited in your note).
summary: Unilever to merge Foods business with McCormick in $44.8bn deal by mid-2027

body:
• Retail volumes described as “record” 65,000 in FY26, +8.0% vs FY25.

• Expects pretax profit of around £7.5m, up 83% vs £4.1m prior year (increase of ~£3.4m).

• In calendar 2025, retail sales volumes +6.0%, vs overall used car market growth +2.2% (outperformance +3.8pp).

• Company statement on AI impact: “Since launching our new agentic artificial intelligence tool to reactivate historic closed quotes, around 900 incremental vehicle sales can be attributed to this channel.”

• Operational notes (per company statement): vehicle supply “more normalised”; “securing more bulk deals”; sourcing more vehicles via “Sell Your Car” channel; data-led buying/pricing “firmly embedded” and supported “record metal margin performance,” helping offset inflationary pressures (notably labour costs).

• New store planned: Leeds opening “during the summer.”

• CEO Mark Carpenter quote: “FY26 has been a step change year for Motorpoint, where the use of data became fundamental within the business and we started to embrace the benefits of AI. Whilst the macroeconomic uncertainty in recent weeks leads to a degree of caution due to the risks of increased inflation and interest rates, our superior customer service, omnichannel business model and exciting growth plans mean we are well placed to take advantage of opportunities to further increase market share and build long term value for shareholders.”

• Annual results timing: to be released in June.

• Share move cited: shares +4.6% to 136.00p.

summary: Portfolio valued at EUR192.6m, down 0.7% QoQ despite strong Rumilly and Stuttgart performance
body:
• Portfolio independently valued at EUR192.6m vs EUR194.0m at 31 Dec (-0.7% QoQ).
  • Valuation at 31 March 2025 also cited as EUR194.0m (implies -0.7% vs that comparator).
  • Rumilly and Stuttgart delivered strong valuation growth attributed to lease extensions.
  • Tenant-related setbacks impacted other assets, particularly Alkmaar and Cannes.
